# PR Quality Gate — Pattern Catalog

Empirical catalog of patterns the GitHub Copilot PR reviewer flags across consuming projects. Each entry is a single markdown table row + JSON `params` per the schema in `README.md` §"Catalog grammar".

This catalog is **project-deidentified**: signatures and patterns are abstract; consuming projects do not need to customize it. FP entries are inline (no separate file).

<!-- Schema reminder (per README.md §Catalog grammar):
     | slug | scope_mode | params | review_pass_only_prompt | fp_slug | tier |
     Pipe characters inside JSON values escaped as \|. Parser unescapes before JSON.parse.
     Tier (6th column) added on lightweight-gate-v5. Values: HIGH | MEDIUM | LOW.
     Legacy 5-cell rows default to tier=MEDIUM with a stderr diagnostic; sunset by next catalog edit. -->

## Tier semantics (added with 6-cell schema bump)

Tier controls cost optimization in lighter panel modes AND `HIGH-TIER-SLUGS.md` generation:

- **HIGH** (rg-rules + review-pass-only): rg-rules run in every mode (full/triage/lint-only); review-pass-only emit `unreviewed_review_pass_only_slugs` warning in lint-only.
- **MEDIUM** (rg-rules + review-pass-only): run in full/triage only; SKIP in lint-only.
- **LOW** (rg-rules + review-pass-only): run in full only; SKIP in triage and lint-only.

Tier auto-derivation by `scripts/sync-critical-rules.ps1`:
- rg-detectable rules (`scope_mode: diff-scoped|tree-scoped|hybrid`): tier from rg-corpus hit count
- review-pass-only rules: tier from `panel-misses.csv` hit count (unique `pr_ref` count, not raw row count)
- Thresholds: ≥5 hits = HIGH, 3-4 = MEDIUM, 0-2 = LOW
- Manual override: row's `tier` column set to explicit value overrides auto-derivation

## Patterns (high-frequency battery, ≥5 hits in seed corpus)

| slug | scope_mode | params | review_pass_only_prompt | fp_slug | tier |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| doc-impl-mismatch | review-pass-only | {} | For every modified source file with XML docs, inline comments, PR description text, or README claims: read each prose claim and verify the surrounding code actually does what the prose says. Common failure modes: XML doc claims thread-context that captured SC contradicts; PR description names a feature the markup does not render; `<see cref>` references that won't resolve; "this delegates to X via COM" when X uses procedural API. **Interface-extension sub-case** (frequently missed; consuming-pr-8 round 3): when an interface or class gets NEW members in the diff (`HasActiveLogs` + `ReloadAllActiveLogsAsync` added to an interface whose summary previously described only the older concern), verify the interface-level `<summary>` enumerates / describes the new members or at least acknowledges the new responsibility surface. A summary that still describes only the pre-extension subset is `doc-impl-mismatch`. **DI-registration-method contract sub-case** (frequently missed; consuming-pr-8 round 7): when a DI registration method (`AddXyz` extension method on `IServiceCollection`, equivalent on `IHostBuilder`, `IApplicationBuilder.UseXyz`, ASP.NET Core `services.AddOptions<T>()`, Autofac `ContainerBuilder.RegisterXyz`, etc.) has an XML summary that documents required external registrations / host-provided dependencies (typical "Callers MUST also register: AddFluxor, AddXxx, ..." pattern), verify that EVERY new singleton/scoped/transient registration in the diff which depends on a host-provided abstraction is reflected in the contract doc. Adding a new registration that depends on `IHostProvidedService` without updating the "callers must register" list means a downstream host calling the registration method gets a DI activation failure at runtime instead of a compile-time / config-time signal. Flag every prose-vs-code divergence. |  | HIGH |
| comment-necessity | review-pass-only | {} | For every comment ADDED in the diff (`//` inline, `/* */` block, `///` XML doc — NOT pre-existing comments in the baseline that happen to live in modified files): verify it clarifies genuinely subtle behavior. **BEFORE adding any comment, evaluate code-clarity refactors first**: would renaming a variable / method / parameter, extracting a helper, or restructuring a conditional eliminate the need for the comment? If yes, refactor instead of commenting. Only comment if the refactor would make the code LESS clear (e.g., name would be too long, extraction would hurt locality). Flag comments that (a) restate what the code obviously does (e.g., `// Increment counter` above `counter++;`), (b) reference panel artifacts (slot numbers, round numbers, PR-bundle labels like `PR 1+2`, panel-phase names, "the bot caught this"), (c) duplicate XML method summaries with inline comments, (d) include multi-line XML `<remarks>` blocks on internal types, (e) narrate refactor/PR history inline, (f) explain a contract that the method/test NAME already conveys (e.g., `// Lock-release invariant` comment above a test named `LockReleasedBetweenCallsAndBothComplete` — name carries intent, comment redundant). Acceptable: brief single-line clarifications for non-obvious *why* (concurrency invariants the code cannot self-document, race-safety rationale, BCL quirks with version citation, FP override citations, spec/standard references); brief `///<summary>` on public APIs. Apply the principle: "Only comment code that needs a bit of clarification. Do not comment otherwise." Use the diff to distinguish NEW comments from baseline. |  | HIGH |
| strongly-typed-id-preference | review-pass-only | {} | For every NEW declaration in the diff that introduces a primitive (`Guid`, `long`, `int`, `string`, `uint`, `ulong`, `short`, `ushort`) as an ID surface, in ANY of these positions: (i) field/property/parameter/local whose name ends with `Id`/`ID`/`id`; (ii) generic type argument that semantically represents an ID (e.g., `Dictionary<long, ModalSession>` where the `long` key is a modal ID; `HashSet<Guid>` of filter IDs; `IDictionary<TKey, TValue>` instantiations where `TKey` is a domain identifier): check whether a strongly-typed ID wrapper (`public readonly record struct XxxId(Guid Value)` or equivalent) already exists in the codebase for this domain. If a wrapper exists, USE IT. If no wrapper exists and this introduces a NEW logical ID domain, prefer creating one following the established codebase pattern (factory `public static XxxId Create() => new(Guid.NewGuid());`). **Acceptable primitives (do NOT flag)**: extensions of an existing primitive-ID subsystem where the surrounding public surface already uses primitives AND switching one site would create inconsistency (the reviewer MUST cite the specific pre-existing primitive ID surface to invoke this carve-out — dodge-by-assertion is not sufficient); EF Core entity row IDs that match the database schema (`long Id`, `int Id`); framework-required types (Blazor `[Parameter] public string Id { get; set; }`, `[Parameter] public Guid SelectorId { get; set; }`); Windows platform IDs (`ushort Id`, `int Id` per platform conventions); interop / P/Invoke handles; correlation/trace/request/diagnostic tokens scoped to single-operation tracing, where the value never crosses domain boundaries. Use the diff to distinguish NEW IDs from extensions of existing primitive-ID code; baseline `long Id` properties on entities are NOT in scope. |  |
| prefer-async-suffix | review-pass-only | {} | For every NEW sync call added in the diff INSIDE a genuinely async method — defined as: the enclosing method declaration has the `async` keyword OR the method body contains `await` somewhere (this excludes sync bridge/adapter methods that merely return `Task`/`Task<T>`/`ValueTask`/`ValueTask<T>` via `Task.CompletedTask`, `Task.FromResult`, or by returning the result of another sync call): verify whether the receiver type has a `<Method>Async` sibling. If yes, prefer the async overload. **If choosing sync where Async exists**, the call MUST be commented with the reason (e.g., `// sync bridge: awaiting captures SynchronizationContext`, `// sync OK: caller holds non-async lock`). Unjustified sync-when-Async-exists is a flag-able violation regardless of the rest of the diff. **High-signal sites to check**: `DbConnection.Open()` / `SqliteConnection.Open()` / `SqlConnection.Open()` → `OpenAsync()` (note: `IDbConnection` itself does NOT define `OpenAsync` — only the concrete/abstract `DbConnection` hierarchy does); `DbContext.SaveChanges()` → `SaveChangesAsync()` (also caught by `async-correctness` rg — DO NOT duplicate-flag if `async-correctness` already surfaced it); `Stream.Dispose()` → `await DisposeAsync()`; `Stream.Read/Write/Flush()` → `Async` versions; `CancellationTokenRegistration.Dispose()` → `DisposeAsync()`; `HttpClient.Send(HttpRequestMessage)` → `SendAsync()` (note: most `HttpClient.*` methods are already `Async`-only — only `Send` is the sync one); `IServiceProvider.GetRequiredService<IDbContextFactory<T>>().CreateDbContext()` → `CreateDbContextAsync()`. **Acceptable (do NOT flag)**: types whose API has no `Async` sibling (`Utf8JsonReader.Read`, `Process.Start`, `ILogger.Debug`, `Math.Sqrt`); sync calls in non-async contexts (`Dispose()` on a `using` statement at end of a sync method — `using` itself handles the dispose form); sync bridge/adapter implementations of an async-shaped contract (e.g., `public Task SaveAsync() { _writer.Flush(); return Task.CompletedTask; }`); explicit diagnostic-only synchronous I/O where the cost is intentional and commented; sites already surfaced by `async-correctness` rg battery — do NOT duplicate-flag. Use the diff to find NEW sync calls in async contexts; do NOT flag pre-existing sync calls in unchanged code. |  | HIGH |
| publication-barrier-order | review-pass-only | {} | For every NEW or modified writer method in the diff that updates multiple fields under a single lock AND publishes one of them via `Volatile.Write` / `Interlocked.Exchange` / `Interlocked.CompareExchange` as a "publication barrier" intended to make the rest visible to cross-thread readers: verify the volatile/interlocked write happens LAST in the write sequence (so it acts as the publication point), NOT first or in the middle. The .NET memory model guarantees writes preceding a `Volatile.Write` (or full-fence `Interlocked` op) are not reordered past it — so any cross-thread reader that observes the volatile-published value is guaranteed to also see the prior writes. If the volatile-write is first/middle, plain-field writes can be reordered past it from a CROSS-THREAD reader's perspective (the lock body's release barrier only fires on `Monitor.Exit`, but cross-thread readers using a DIFFERENT lock or no lock can observe the partial state during the writer's lock body). **High-signal patterns to check**: `Volatile.Write(ref _activeId, newId); ActiveType = newType;` (id published BEFORE state) — wrong; should be `ActiveType = newType; Volatile.Write(ref _activeId, newId);` (id LAST). Mirror for clear paths: clear plain fields first, then volatile-write sentinel id LAST. Also flag `_idCounter++; Volatile.Write(ref _activeId, _idCounter);` followed by plain writes — same issue, reorder so id-write is last. **Acceptable (do NOT flag)**: lock-protected reads via the SAME lock as the writer (mutual exclusion makes ordering irrelevant); writer-side-only state with no cross-thread readers; explicit `Interlocked.MemoryBarrier()` / `Thread.MemoryBarrier()` between writes (rare, only when the volatile-write semantics aren't sufficient). Apply to writer paths only; reader-side double-read patterns (re-read the volatile after plain reads) are a complementary concern, not this rule. |  |
| event-ordering-test-subscription | review-pass-only | {} | For every NEW test in the diff that asserts ORDERING between an EVENT (e.g., `StateChanged?.Invoke()`, `PropertyChanged`, `INotifyPropertyChanged.PropertyChanged`) and a SUBJECT-ACTION (e.g., a TCS completion that resumes an awaiter, a state transition observable by the consumer): verify the test's event subscription happens AFTER any non-asserted setup action that fires the same event. Subscribing BEFORE a setup `Show()`/`Initialize()`/`Open()`/`Start()` that ALSO fires the event causes the SETUP event to set the assertion flag, masking whether the SUBJECT-ACTION-fired event actually preceded the awaiter. The test then passes regardless of correctness — a false-positive risk that defeats the test's purpose. **High-signal pattern**: ``sut.StateChanged += () => { if (!awaiterResumed) flag = true; }; sut.Show<...>(); ... sut.CancelActive();`` — the `Show` event sets `flag=true` before `CancelActive` runs. **Fix options**: (a) move `+= ...` AFTER the setup `Show()`, (b) count event invocations and assert on the Nth, (c) reset the flag between setup and subject-action. **Acceptable (do NOT flag)**: tests where the event is wired up via DI/constructor and there's no separable setup-vs-subject phase; tests asserting "event fires at least once" rather than "event fires before X"; tests where the setup-fired event and the subject-fired event are distinguishable by some other property (e.g., the event argument carries identity). Use the diff to find NEW tests; pre-existing test patterns with the same shape are not in scope. |  | HIGH |
| event-completion-fault-isolation | review-pass-only | {} | For every NEW or modified writer method in the diff that BOTH (a) invokes a public event (e.g., `StateChanged?.Invoke()`, `PropertyChanged?.Invoke(...)`, custom multi-cast delegate) OR a caller-supplied callback (`Action`, `Func<T>`, `Action<T>`, custom delegate parameter) AND (b) subsequently completes a `TaskCompletionSource`, invokes a captured cancel delegate, or otherwise resumes an awaiting caller: verify the post-event completion is wrapped in `try/finally` so the completion runs even when the subscriber/callback throws. Pattern that BREAKS: ``StateChanged?.Invoke(); tcs.TrySetResult(value);`` — if any subscriber throws, the TCS never completes and the awaiter hangs forever. Same hang risk with caller callbacks: ``onComplete?.Invoke(); tcs.TrySetResult(value);``. Pattern that WORKS: ``try { StateChanged?.Invoke(); } finally { tcs.TrySetResult(value); }`` — TCS completion is guaranteed regardless of subscriber exceptions; the exception still propagates to the caller for diagnostics. This applies to ALL completion shapes: `TrySetResult`, `TrySetCanceled`, `TrySetException`, `cancelDelegate?.Invoke()` capturing a TCS, `IPromise.Resolve`, etc. **Acceptable (do NOT flag)**: event/callback invocations with no follow-up completion (purely notification); event invocations where the completion semantically depends on subscriber outcome (rare; document if so); event/callback invocations that follow rather than precede completion (e.g., `tcs.TrySetResult(value); StateChanged?.Invoke();` — but verify this ordering is intentional, since it can violate the publication-before-resume discipline that subscribers often rely on). Use the diff to find NEW writer paths; pre-existing event-then-complete patterns in unchanged code are not in scope. |  | HIGH |
| test-aaa-pattern | review-pass-only | {} | For every NEW test method (`[Fact]` or `[Theory]`) added in the diff: verify the test body is structured with `// Arrange`, `// Act`, and `// Assert` markers separating the three phases (the AAA pattern). This convention is enforced across the codebase's existing test files and improves readability + reviewability. **Apply when**: a test has a discernible setup → action → verification flow. The markers must be on their own lines as comments (e.g., `        // Arrange`); inline comments don't count. **Acceptable variations (do NOT flag)**: tests with no Act phase (pure state assertions on initial construction) may use `// Arrange` + `// Assert` only — drop `// Act` rather than insert a placeholder; tests that mix Act+Assert (e.g., `Assert.Throws<T>(() => sut.Method())`) may combine via a single `// Act + Assert` marker; data-only `[Theory]` rows that exercise multiple cases in a loop may use the markers around the loop, not within. **NOT flagged**: pre-existing tests in modified files that lacked AAA before this PR (not in scope — AAA-converting baseline tests is separate cleanup work); micro-benchmarks; helper test methods that aren't `[Fact]`/`[Theory]`; generated/source-generated test files (files with `<auto-generated>` headers, files inside `obj/` or generator output directories, or files where the generator owns the layout — adding AAA markers to generator output is not a valid remediation). Use the diff to find NEW hand-authored tests; mirror the existing codebase convention. |  |
| panel-artifact-leakage | diff-scoped | {"pattern":"\\b(Slot \\d+\|R\\d+ (finding\|fix\|round\|rework\|ready)\|PR \\d+\\+\\d+\|pre-(implementation\|PR-creation) panel\|round[ -]?\\d+\|bot.*(caught\|finding\|flagged))\\b","glob":["*.cs"]} |  |  | HIGH |
| resource-cleanup | hybrid | {"tree":{"pattern":"new\\s+(CancellationTokenSource\|SqliteConnection\|SqlConnection\|FileStream\|StreamReader\|StreamWriter\|HttpClient\|ProcessStartInfo)\\(","glob":["*.cs"]},"diff":{"pattern":"new\\s+(CancellationTokenSource\|SqliteConnection\|SqlConnection\|FileStream\|StreamReader\|StreamWriter\|HttpClient\|ProcessStartInfo)\\(","glob":["*.cs"]}} |  |  |
| naming-convention | diff-scoped | {"pattern":"^\\s+var [A-Z][a-z]","glob":["tests/**/*.cs"]} |  |  |
| async-correctness | hybrid | {"tree":{"pattern":"SaveChanges\\(\\)\|_ = InvokeAsync\|\\.Result\\b\|\\.Wait\\(\\)","glob":["*.cs"]},"diff":{"pattern":"SaveChanges\\(\\)\|_ = InvokeAsync\|\\.Result\\b\|\\.Wait\\(\\)","glob":["*.cs"]}} |  |  |
| thread-safety | tree-scoped | {"pattern":"IProgress<.*>\|new Progress<","glob":["*.cs"]} |  |  |
| bounds-empty-collection | diff-scoped | {"pattern":"\\b\\w+\\[0\\]\|stackalloc \\w+\\[\\w+\\.Length\\]","glob":["*.cs"]} |  |  |
| aria-binding | diff-scoped | {"pattern":"aria-\|_is[A-Z]\\w+\\s*=\\s*(true\|false\|!_)","glob":["*.razor","*.razor.cs"]} |  |  |
| regex-validation | tree-scoped | {"pattern":"new Regex\\(\|filter.*regex\|regex.*filter","glob":["*.cs","*.razor"]} |  | fp-1 |
| razor-binding | hybrid | {"tree":{"pattern":":empty","glob":["*.css"]},"diff":{"pattern":"@onscroll\|@onkeydown\|@onkeyup\|(public\|protected) (async )?(void\|Task) (Show\|Hide\|Toggle\|Open\|Close)\\w*","glob":["*.razor","*.razor.cs"]}} |  |  |
| null-handling | diff-scoped | {"pattern":"\"\\[.*\\{[^}]*\\}\\]\"","glob":["*.cs"]} |  |  |
| dead-code-unused | review-pass-only | {} | For every NEW or modified `.cs` / `.ts` / `.tsx` / `.js` / `.jsx` file in the diff: identify symbols introduced by the PR that have no incoming reference. Common cases: a private method called by only one site that the same PR removes (the method is now dead); a parameter added to a method that the body never reads; a local variable assigned but never observed; an enum value with no producer or consumer; an import/using statement added for a type the PR no longer references; a removed `if` branch that leaves a captured local unused. Flag the dead symbol and propose deletion. **Acceptable (do NOT flag)**: public API surface (callers may live in other repos / out-of-tree consumers); test-only helpers added in the same PR that the test file itself uses; symbols guarded behind `[Conditional("DEBUG")]` / `#if` directives; designer-generated members; framework conventions that look unused but are reflected over (e.g., Blazor `[Parameter]`, Fluxor `[FeatureState]`, EF Core navigation properties, `[JsonPropertyName]`-decorated DTOs, MSTest/xUnit attribute-discovered methods). Use the diff to find the NEW symbols; do not regress on legacy unused code that predates this PR. |  |
| null-dereference-risk | review-pass-only | {} | For every NEW receiver-access (`obj.Member`, `obj[index]`, `obj()`, `obj!.Member`, optional chaining `obj?.Member.X` where `.X` is non-null-safe) added in the diff: verify the receiver cannot be `null` at that program point. Specifically: track null-conditional propagation (`a?.B.C` — if `a` is null then `.B` is null then `.C` NREs), parameter origins for nullable-annotated parameters consumed unguarded, fields declared without initializer that can race null-default with init, return-value chains where an upstream method can return null. **Acceptable (do NOT flag)**: receivers already guarded by `if (x is null) return` / `ArgumentNullException.ThrowIfNull(x)` / `Debug.Assert(x is not null)` immediately above; non-nullable annotated locals/parameters with no flow that could produce null (full compiler NRT analysis suffices — do not double-flag what the compiler already enforces); `!` null-forgiving operator on a receiver where the surrounding code has just type-tested or pattern-matched. Use the diff to identify NEW receiver accesses; pre-existing potential-null patterns in unchanged code are not in scope. |  |
| code-comment-mismatch | review-pass-only | {} | For every NEW inline comment (`//`) or block comment (`/* */`) on a NEW or MODIFIED line in the diff: verify the comment text matches what the adjacent code now does. Distinct from `doc-impl-mismatch` (which covers prose claims in XML docs / PR description / README): this rule targets the COMMENT-TO-CODE drift where a prior comment was preserved through a refactor that changed the surrounding logic. **High-signal patterns**: `// Create TestButton` above `var x = new Label("TestLabel");` (creates Label, not Button); `// Deadlock on iOS .NET 10` above code that does not exhibit that deadlock; `// Cancel and dispose` above a method body that no longer disposes; `// Returns null when X` above a method whose new body always returns a default; `// 5 seconds` above `TimeSpan.FromMilliseconds(3000)`. **Extraction-drift sub-case** (frequently missed; consuming-pr-8 round 4): when a helper method (or interface, or class) is EXTRACTED from a larger method in the diff, comments that traveled with the extracted code often describe operations the new location does NOT perform — e.g., a catch-block comment saying "torn down between the _disposed check and the dispatcher call" preserved into an extracted helper whose body has only the dispatcher call (no preceding _disposed check). For every NEW method body whose code matches a pattern that previously lived in a larger method (extraction signature: new private/internal method whose body is a shorter recombination of code from another method in the diff), audit each comment for references to variables, local guards, or sibling operations that aren't present in the extracted scope. Flag every drift. **Acceptable (do NOT flag)**: comments that describe *general intent* and remain accurate even if implementation detail changes (e.g., `// Compute the running total` above any aggregate); comments explaining a non-obvious *why* (concurrency invariant, race-safety) that the code still upholds; comments referencing a spec/standard that the code conforms to. Use the diff to identify comments whose adjacent code changed; pre-existing comments on baseline-unmodified lines are not in scope. |  | HIGH |
| behavior-suppressed-by-guard | review-pass-only | {} | For every NEW early-return / `if (...) return;` / `if (...) return null;` / `if (...) throw;` / `if (...) { ... return; }` guard added at the top of a public or virtual method body: verify the guard does not silently suppress a code path the prior implementation supported. **High-signal patterns**: `if (Window is null) return;` added to a method that previously queued the request and replayed it after `Window` was assigned (the early-return now drops legitimate queued calls); `if (!_initialized) return;` added to a method whose callers depend on it executing even pre-init; `if (cancellationToken.IsCancellationRequested) return;` at the entry of a finalizer/cleanup method (cleanup must run regardless of cancellation). Flag the guard and propose: (a) inversion to a precondition exception, (b) deferred queueing instead of dropping, or (c) explicit no-op-with-trace if silent-drop is intended. **Acceptable (do NOT flag)**: guards added with explicit log/telemetry surfacing the early-return; guards whose dropped path is provably equivalent to the post-guard path (e.g., `if (list.Count == 0) return;` before a foreach that would no-op anyway); ArgumentException-throwing guards on invalid input. Use the diff to find NEW guards; pre-existing guards that simply moved around are not in scope. |  |
| file-path-injection-traversal | review-pass-only | {} | For every NEW `Path.Combine` / `Path.GetFullPath` / `File.*` / `Directory.*` / `FileStream(...)` / `new StreamReader(path)` / shell-out / `string.Format("...{0}", path)` call added in the diff whose path argument derives from external input (`HttpRequest`, query string, posted form, JSON body, environment variable, command-line argument, file upload, websocket message, `[Parameter]` from URL): verify the path is canonicalized AND validated against a `..` traversal AND constrained to an allow-list of base directories. **High-signal failure**: `var p = Path.Combine(baseDir, userInput); File.ReadAllText(p);` — `userInput = "../../etc/passwd"` escapes `baseDir`. Required defense: compute `Path.GetFullPath(p)` and assert it starts-with the canonicalized base. **Acceptable (do NOT flag)**: path arguments that are static / compile-time constants; paths from a configuration value the operator owns (still flag if the config source is user-writable); paths under a per-user scratch directory where traversal can only escape into another user-writable area; OS-level chroot/sandbox enforced before the path is consumed (cite the sandbox). Use the diff to find NEW path-consuming sites with external-input lineage; existing already-validated paths are not in scope. |  |
| config-default-missing | review-pass-only | {} | For every NEW `Configuration[key]` / `IConfiguration.GetValue<T>(key)` / `Environment.GetEnvironmentVariable(key)` / `_config.GetSection(key)` / `process.env.KEY` / `ConfigurationManager.AppSettings[key]` read added in the diff: verify the consuming code documents and handles the missing-key case. **Required**: either (a) `?? defaultValue` on the read with the default named in source or a doc comment, (b) `?? throw new InvalidOperationException("ConfigKey \"X\" is required")` if it must be set, or (c) an explicit upstream presence-check in the same PR (e.g., a startup validator that fails fast). **High-signal failure**: `var connStr = _config["ConnectionStrings:Db"]; new SqlConnection(connStr);` — passes `null` to the connection constructor and throws NRE far from the misconfiguration; reviewer cannot trace cause. **Acceptable (do NOT flag)**: configuration consumed via Options pattern bound at startup where the binder already throws on missing required properties (`[Required]` data annotation, `services.AddOptions<T>().ValidateDataAnnotations()`); test-only configuration with hardcoded fallbacks; configuration keys whose absence is semantically valid (feature-flag opt-in). Use the diff to find NEW config reads; pre-existing patterns are not in scope. |  |
| typo-naming-inconsistency | review-pass-only | {} | For every NEW identifier (type name, member name, parameter, local, file name, route segment, JSON property name, GitHub Actions step id, secret key) added in the diff: scan the surrounding file (within 200 lines or the same class/module) for adjacent identifiers that follow a consistent stem. Flag identifiers that break the stem: `OnFooChanged` adjacent to `OnBarChange` (Changed vs Change); `databaseId` parameter where the surrounding API uses `dbId`; `[Parameter] public string OperationId` where the rest of the component uses `RequestId`; CSS class `.btn-primary` adjacent to `.button-secondary`; route `/api/customer` adjacent to `/api/customers`. Also flag plain typos: `Recieve`, `Calender`, `Lable`, `Seperate`, `Occured`, double-letter mistakes (`acommodate`, `addres`). **Acceptable (do NOT flag)**: language-required spellings (color vs colour — match the surrounding repo convention; `Color` is canonical .NET); intentional version-bump renames (`V2`, `New`, `Legacy` suffixes); identifiers documented in an external API contract whose spelling we cannot change. Use the diff to find NEW identifiers; pre-existing naming drift in baseline code is not in scope. |  |
| attribute-or-decorator-missing | review-pass-only | {} | For every NEW class / method / property / parameter added in the diff that participates in a framework convention requiring a marker attribute: verify the attribute is present. **High-signal cases**: ASP.NET Core controller action method without `[HttpGet]`/`[HttpPost]`/etc. when the routing requires explicit verbs; Blazor `[Parameter]` missing on a public property a parent component is supposed to bind; EF Core model property whose column requires `[Required]` / `[MaxLength]` / `[Column(TypeName = "...")]` to match the schema; xUnit `[Theory]` without `[InlineData]` / `[MemberData]` / `[ClassData]`; `[Authorize]` missing on a controller action that handles sensitive data when the rest of the controller is authorized; Fluxor `[FeatureState]` / `[ReducerMethod]` missing on state classes / reducer methods; serialization attributes (`[JsonPropertyName]`, `[XmlElement]`) missing where the surrounding API uses them consistently. Flag the missing attribute and cite the framework convention. **Acceptable (do NOT flag)**: convention-by-name routing (ASP.NET Core attribute-less methods that follow `[controller]/[action]` patterns where the surrounding controller does the same); test-only helper classes that don\u0027t participate in the framework; non-public members not exposed to the framework. Use the diff to find NEW framework-participating declarations. |  |
| localization-i18n-gap | review-pass-only | {} | For every NEW user-visible string added in the diff — defined as: a `string` literal passed to a UI-rendering API (Blazor `@text`, MAUI `<Label Text="...">`, MAUI `DisplayAlert`, `IAlertDialogService.ShowAlert`, `Console.WriteLine` in CLI tools meant for end-users, exception messages thrown to user-facing layers, `TraceLogger.Information` if log surfaces to UI), OR a string in a `.razor` markup line that renders directly: verify the string goes through the project\u0027s localization mechanism (`.resx`, `IStringLocalizer<T>`, `@inject IStringLocalizer<T>`, custom resource catalog) — not hard-coded. **High-signal failure**: `<Label Text="An operation is running. Cancel and close anyway?">` — non-English users see English. **Acceptable (do NOT flag)**: debug log strings (`TraceLogger.Debug`) that surface only to developers; exception messages whose audience is developers reading stack traces; system-language-locked strings (date formats handled by `CultureInfo`); strings that ARE the canonical name of a concept (`"SettingsModal"` as a key, `"db"` as a tab id) — these are identifiers, not user-visible text; placeholders/test-data in tests. Use the diff to find NEW UI-rendered strings; pre-existing non-localized strings in baseline are not in scope (separate localization sweep). |  |
| race-condition-thread-safety | review-pass-only | {} | For every NEW shared mutable state added in the diff (instance field, static field, class-level collection) that is read OR written from MORE THAN ONE thread / async continuation / callback: verify access is synchronized. | await-throwable-async-call-without-handler-in-teardown-flow | review-pass-only | {} | For every NEW `await <async-method-that-can-throw>` in the diff inside a tear-down / dispose / save-and-close / modal-close / `OnDeactivating` / `OnBeforeUnload` / `IAsyncDisposable.DisposeAsync` / equivalent close-flow handler: verify exceptions are caught and either (a) routed to a banner/log surface, or (b) explicitly swallowed for known-transient cases (cancellation, timeout) WITH at minimum a `ITraceLogger.Warning` / `_logger.Warning($"... {ex}")` call so the failure is not invisible. For each, verify the enclosing try/catch covers the call's documented exception set AND emits at least one log line per non-OCE swallow. |  | HIGH |

| deferred-throw-resets-stack-trace-use-exceptiondispatchinfo | review-pass-only | {} | For every NEW pattern in the diff that captures an exception in a local variable and rethrows it later via `throw <varName>;` — typically in a deferred-throw / catch-and-defer / capture-then-cleanup flow: replace `Exception? deferredEx = null; ... catch (Exception ex) { deferredEx = ex; ... } ... if (deferredEx is not null) { throw deferredEx; }` with `ExceptionDispatchInfo? deferredEx = null; ... catch (Exception ex) { deferredEx = ExceptionDispatchInfo.Capture(ex); ... } ... deferredEx?.Throw();`. **Why this matters**: `throw <variable>;` is semantically a NEW throw — CLR resets `Exception.StackTrace` to the current `throw` site, losing the trace from the original throw point. Operators investigating "TimeoutException at line N" see line N as the deferred-throw site, NOT the actual `WaitAsync` site that timed out. **`ExceptionDispatchInfo.Capture(ex).Throw()`** preserves the original throw point via the EDI's internal stack-trace token. **High-signal failure (cited from consuming-pr-8 round 13)**: round-12 added deferred-throw + best-effort reopen to `ReloadAllActiveLogsAsync` via `Exception? deferredCloseFailure = null; ... throw deferredCloseFailure;` — preserving operability of UI on close failure, but losing diagnostic context. **Distinct from bare `throw;`** (rethrow inside the same catch block): bare `throw;` DOES preserve stack trace; this rule only targets stored-variable rethrows. **Distinct from re-wrapping**: `throw new MyException("wrapper", ex);` is a different pattern (deliberate wrapping with InnerException — that DOES preserve original via InnerException). **Acceptable (do NOT flag)**: bare `throw;` rethrows; `throw new XxxException(..., ex);` wrappers where the inner exception is the original; sites using `ExceptionDispatchInfo` already; test code that deliberately constructs and throws fresh exceptions (no original to preserve). **Audit method**: regex-grep `Exception\\??\\s+\\w+\\s*=\\s*(null\|ex)\\b` in NEW code paths; for each match, locate the subsequent `throw <varName>;` and replace with EDI capture + Throw pattern. | razor-lambda-eventcallback-drops-task | review-pass-only | {} | For every NEW Razor block lambda or expression lambda assigned to an event handler attribute (`@onclick`, `@onchange`, `@onkeydown`, `@oninput`, `@onsubmit`, etc.) in a `.razor` file whose BODY invokes `EventCallback.InvokeAsync()` / `EventCallback<T>.InvokeAsync(arg)` / `Task SomethingAsync()` / any `Task`-returning expression: verify the lambda either (a) uses `async` + `await` so the returned Task is observed by Blazor's event dispatcher, OR (b) the parent assigns the EventCallback directly without a wrapping lambda (e.g., `@onclick="OnX"` where `OnX` is itself an `EventCallback` / `EventCallback<MouseEventArgs>`), OR (c) the lambda explicitly observes the Task via `.ContinueWith(...OnlyOnFaulted)` / discards with a documented "best-effort" comment. **High-signal failure (cited from consuming-pr-10 round 1 — bot finding on PRs #553 + #555)**: `@onclick="@(() => OnRetryClassification.InvokeAsync())"` — block lambda returns `void` (Action type) so the `Task` returned by `InvokeAsync()` is silently discarded; subscriber exceptions become unobserved; Blazor completes the event dispatch before the callback runs. | stacked-pr-fix-propagation-gap-during-rebase | review-pass-only | {} | **PROCESS RULE.** For every stack of PRs (foundation → polish → feature) where a defect found in the foundation PR is fixed on a HIGHER PR in the stack via a fix commit that is NOT cherry-picked back down to the foundation: when the foundation PR is reviewed again, the same defect will be flagged because the foundation tree does not contain the fix. The fix must be applied to the LOWEST PR in the stack whose tree contains the defect, then rebased upward. **Why this matters**: stacked PRs share file lineage. Fixing a defect on the upper PR's branch (because that's where the agent is currently working) creates a "fix-propagation gap" — the foundation PR still ships the bug, the bot re-flags it on the next review, and the agent has to fix it twice. This is especially common when the bot reviews the foundation PR in a later round AFTER the agent has already addressed the same defect on the upper PR's branch (the agent's mental model is "fixed in PR X, done", but each PR is reviewed against its OWN tree). **High-signal failure (cited from consuming-pr-10 round 3 — PR #553 re-flagged `DatabaseEntryRow.razor:58` + `:71` unawaited lambdas)**: PR #553's foundation includes Commit B (`c8fc3bcf` "Improve database entry row affordances") which added Retry + RestoreFromBackup buttons with block-lambda click handlers. The bot flagged these in PR #553 round 3. The agent's round-1 fix `5a359257` ("Awaited InvokeAsync in Retry Upgrade and Restore From Backup click lambdas") landed on `jschick/modal-coordinator-pr10-bcd` (PR #555) instead of `jschick/modal-coordinator-pr9` (PR #553), because the agent was iterating on the stack from the top down. When PR #553 was reviewed again, the bot saw the unfixed lambdas in pr9's tree. **Required workflow**: (1) when classifying a bot finding, identify the LOWEST PR in the stack whose tree contains the defective code (`git log <branch> -- <file>` to find the introducing commit, then map commit → PR); (2) apply the fix to THAT PR's branch; (3) rebase upper PRs onto the fix; (4) push all affected branches. **Acceptable (do NOT flag)**: fixes that are scope-locked to a specific upper PR (e.g., a fix that only applies to a feature added in the upper PR — not present in the foundation tree); cases where the lower PR was already merged before the finding surfaced. **Audit method**: every fix commit on a stacked-PR branch should be paired with a check via `git log <foundation-branch> -- <file>` to confirm the defect is NOT also present in the lower branch's tree; if it is, the fix must land on the lower branch first then rebase. |  | HIGH |

| implementation-to-commit-transition-bypasses-user-approval | review-pass-only | {} | **PROCESS RULE.** At the implementation → git transition (tests green, lint clean, optionally PR-quality-gate `core_rules_acknowledged` block emitted), the FIRST tool call MUST be `ask_user` for diff approval — NOT `git add`. PR-quality-gate ack passing does NOT satisfy the §0 user-approval gate; the two gates are DISTINCT (see `AGENTS.md` §0 dual-gate callout). **Applies to ALL commit-producing operations**: bare `git commit`, `git commit --amend` (interactive or `-m`), `git rebase --continue` after edit/conflict resolution, `git cherry-pick` (incl. `--continue` after conflicts), `git am` / `git am --continue`, `git commit-tree` direct, `git merge --no-ff --commit` (when not fast-forward), and `git revert` (since revert produces a new commit). PR-comment-driven amendments are NOT routine — each amend is a NEW commit-producing event and requires its own diff-approval `ask_user`. The rationale "the user already approved the original commit" does NOT carry forward to amends that change its content. **Why this matters**: the most common bypass pattern across multiple consuming-pr-N cycles is the agent's internal monologue "tests passed + ack done → ready to commit"; the agent treats catalog ack as commit-license and skips the human-in-the-loop step entirely. **Required workflow**: (1) implementation completes (tests, lint, post-impl review); (2) agent emits the literal `PRE-GIT SENTINEL` block (per `AGENTS.md` §0) declaring `user_diff_approval: pending`; (3) NEXT tool call is `ask_user` for diff approval, showing the staged diff or working-tree diff verbatim; (4) AFTER user approval received with verbatim phrase, emit `PRE-COMMIT GATE PASSED` block (per `pre-commit.md`) including the verbatim approval phrase in `diff_approved`; (5) THEN `git add` → `git commit` → (if `ask_user` re-confirms) `git push`. **High-signal failures**: consuming-pr-6 `amend-path` sub-case (agent treated `git commit --amend` + `git push --force-with-lease` after PR-comment fix as routine and skipped the block; user pushback "we have done 2-3 amends related to this"); consuming-pr-8 `post-impl-review-skipped-after-pre-impl-panel-ready` (agent chained pre-impl-panel READY directly to commit without post-impl review or user approval); consuming-pr-9 (agent finished Phase 1 test, ran tests green, executed `git add` → `git commit` → `git push --force-with-lease` to a new branch without ever calling `ask_user`; user response: "never gave approval to git add or git commit, why are we doing this again, please fix the instructions"). Each occurrence reveals the same root cause: the agent does not pause at the implementation → git boundary because catalog ack feels like sufficient gate-passage. **Acceptable (do NOT flag)**: commit / push tool calls where a `PRE-GIT SENTINEL` block appears earlier in the same turn / recent conversation AND a verbatim user-approval phrase is recorded in the `PRE-COMMIT GATE PASSED` block's `diff_approved` field; cases where the user proactively granted approval without being asked (the user's proactive phrase still records in `diff_approved`); session-resume cases where the prior session committed but the new session is a no-op review only (no new commit); `git commit --amend --no-edit` that is mechanically restoring a previously-emitted committer timestamp without changing tree or message (e.g., immediately after a successful `git rebase --continue`) — the original commit's `PRE-COMMIT GATE PASSED` block is preserved by reference (per `pre-commit.md` Skip conditions). **Audit method**: in any turn that calls `git add` / `git commit` (any flag including `--amend`) / `git rebase --continue` / `git cherry-pick` (any flag including `--continue`) / `git am` (any flag) / `git commit-tree` / `git revert` / `git push` / `git push --force-with-lease` / `git push --force`, locate the most recent prior tool call. If it is NOT an `ask_user` call returning an approval phrase AND no `PRE-GIT SENTINEL` block appears in the agent's text output earlier in the conversation since the implementation phase began OR since the most recent commit-producing event, the §0 gate was bypassed. Process violation; track in `panel-misses.csv` under this slug. |  | HIGH |
| magic-string-numeric-literal | review-pass-only | {} | For every NEW string or numeric literal added in the diff appearing in a non-test code path that controls program behavior (compared in a switch, passed as a config key, used as a route segment, used as a timeout, used as a buffer size, used as a retry count, used as a port number, used as a magic byte sentinel): verify the literal is either (a) declared as a named constant (`const`/`readonly static`) with intent-documenting name, (b) sourced from configuration with a documented default, or (c) genuinely so small/simple it would be more confusing as a constant (0, 1, -1, "", `string.Empty`). **High-signal failure**: `Task.Delay(3000)` — what does 3000 represent? Should be `Task.Delay(StartupSettleDelayMilliseconds)` or `Task.Delay(TimeSpan.FromSeconds(3))`. Similarly: `if (status == "InProgress")` should be `if (status == StatusValues.InProgress)`. **Acceptable (do NOT flag)**: literals in tests (test data, expected values, parametrized cases); literals in resource-key constants files; mathematical literals (0, 1, -1, π, e, 100 for percent); error message format strings ("Failed to open {0}"); literals that ARE the value being defined (i.e., the constant declaration site itself). Use the diff to find NEW behavior-controlling literals; pre-existing magic values in baseline are not in scope. |  |
| razor-css-isolation-cross-file-class-reference | review-pass-only | {} | For every NEW or modified `.razor` markup file that references a CSS class name in its `class="..."` attributes: verify the class name is either (a) defined in the SAME component's `.razor.css` file (scope-isolated styles), or (b) defined in a global CSS file under `wwwroot/` / `_content/`, or (c) explicitly declared with the `::deep` combinator from a parent component's `.razor.css`. **High-signal failure (cited from PR #558 round 4)**: `ManageDatabasesTab.razor` uses class names `db-entry-checkbox` and `db-entry-checkbox--visible`, but those styles are defined in `DatabaseEntryRow.razor.css` (a SIBLING component's scope-isolated stylesheet). Blazor's CSS isolation appends a per-component `[b-XXX]` attribute selector to every rule in the sibling's `.razor.css` — the rules apply only to elements RENDERED by `DatabaseEntryRow.razor`, not by `ManageDatabasesTab.razor`. Result: the markup compiles, the page renders, no warning emits, but the styles silently don't apply. **Fix patterns**: (a) move the shared rules to a global CSS file under `wwwroot/css/`; (b) duplicate the rules into BOTH components' `.razor.css` files (acceptable when the components have genuinely-separate concerns); (c) use the `::deep` combinator in a parent component's `.razor.css` if the parent renders both children (e.g., a `ModalChrome.razor.css` with `::deep .db-entry-checkbox { ... }` styling its descendants); (d) refactor the markup so the styled element lives in the owning component (move the checkbox markup INTO `DatabaseEntryRow.razor`). **Acceptable (do NOT flag)**: class names defined in the same file's `.razor.css`; class names defined in a global stylesheet (`<link>` tag, `wwwroot/css/`); class names with the `::deep` combinator confirmed in a parent's `.razor.css`; framework-provided class names (Bootstrap's `btn`, Tailwind's `flex`, MudBlazor's `mud-button`). **Audit method**: for every NEW `class="<value>"` attribute in a modified `.razor` file, extract each class token. For each token, grep the SAME file's `.razor.css` first; if not found, grep `wwwroot/**/*.css` for the token; if not found, grep parent components' `.razor.css` for `::deep .<token>`; if still not found AND the token doesn't match a framework prefix, flag. |  | HIGH |
| aria-disabled-without-disabled-attribute | review-pass-only | {} | For every NEW or modified interactive element (`<button>`, `<a>`, `<input>`, `<select>`, `<textarea>`, custom Razor button component) in `.razor` / `.html` / `.cshtml` files that has `aria-disabled="true"` or `aria-disabled="@expr"` set: verify the element ALSO has the actual `disabled` HTML attribute (for native form controls) OR a click-handler guard that early-returns on the same condition. `aria-disabled` is an ACCESSIBILITY signal only — it tells screen readers "this is disabled" but does NOT prevent keyboard focus, click events, or form submission. Without the actual `disabled` attribute (or a guard), sighted keyboard / mouse users can still trigger the action. **High-signal failure (cited from PR #558 round 1)**: `<button class="btn-primary" aria-disabled="@IsUpgradeBlocked" @onclick="OnBulkUpgradeClickAsync">Upgrade @SelectedCount</button>` — `aria-disabled` set but the button has no `disabled` attribute and `OnBulkUpgradeClickAsync` doesn't early-return on `IsUpgradeBlocked`. Sighted user clicks the visually-disabled button; the click handler runs anyway. **Fix patterns**: (a) add the actual `disabled` attribute alongside aria-disabled: `<button disabled="@IsUpgradeBlocked" aria-disabled="@IsUpgradeBlocked" ...>` (works for native `<button>` / `<input>`); (b) early-return in the click handler: `if (IsUpgradeBlocked) return;` (acceptable but loses focus-disable semantic — keyboard user can still Tab onto it); (c) for custom Razor button components, plumb the disabled-ness through to the underlying `<button>` (don't render the `@onclick` at all when disabled). **Acceptable (do NOT flag)**: elements with both `aria-disabled` AND `disabled` set (correct pattern); elements where the click handler genuinely IS guarded with the same predicate (verify by reading the handler body — handler-side guard is weaker than `disabled` but defensible if focus-tab semantics are intentional); non-interactive elements with `aria-disabled` (e.g., a `<div role="button">` that's part of a custom widget where the role layer manages all interactions). **Audit method**: grep `aria-disabled\s*=` in NEW or modified `.razor` / `.html` / `.cshtml` files. For each match, verify the same element ALSO declares `disabled\s*=` OR the on-click handler's first statement early-returns on the same expression. If neither, flag. |  | HIGH |
| readonly-interface-leaks-backing-mutable-collection | review-pass-only | {} | For every NEW or modified public/internal API surface in the diff that exposes a property, return value, event-args field, or test-fake-stored value typed as a read-only collection interface (`IReadOnlyList<T>`, `IReadOnlyCollection<T>`, `IReadOnlyDictionary<K,V>`, `IReadOnlySet<T>`, `IEnumerable<T>` used as a "read-only" contract): verify the BACKING storage cannot be downcast by a consumer to mutate it. **Hard truth — what IS and is NOT immutable**: a consumer holding `IReadOnlyList<T>` CAN cast to `T[]` (and mutate elements) when the backing is an array, AND CAN cast to `List<T>` (and call `.Add` / `.RemoveAt`) when the backing is a `List<T>`. The ONLY truly downcast-safe options are: `ImmutableArray<T>` / `ImmutableList<T>` / `FrozenSet<T>` / `ImmutableDictionary<K,V>` (immutable storage; casts to mutable types fail at runtime), or `ReadOnlyCollection<T>` via `.AsReadOnly()` (a wrapper that throws `NotSupportedException` on mutation). Three known leak patterns: (a) **direct backing-field return** — `public IReadOnlyList<T> Items => _items;` where `_items` is a `List<T>` field. A caller does `((List<T>)x.Items).Add(...)` and mutates the producer's state. Fix: return `_items.AsReadOnly()` (wraps to throw on cast-mutate) OR change the backing to `ImmutableArray<T>` AND expose it typed as `ImmutableArray<T>` (not `IReadOnlyList<T>` — exposing immutable storage through a read-only interface signals "callers may try to downcast"; expose the immutable type directly). (b) **event args holding caller-supplied collection** — `public sealed record StartedEventArgs(IReadOnlyList<string> FileNames);` populated via `new StartedEventArgs(callerList)` (no copy at all — caller mutates the list, subscribers see mutation) OR `new StartedEventArgs(callerList.ToList())` (defensive copy of references, but the COPY is a `List<string>` that subscribers can still downcast). Fix: `new StartedEventArgs(ImmutableArray.CreateRange(callerList))` AND change the record's field type to `ImmutableArray<string>` so the contract is truly immutable. (c) **test fake stores caller's collection** — `public List<IReadOnlyList<string>> UpgradeBatchCalls { get; } = new(); public Task X(IReadOnlyList<string> names) { UpgradeBatchCalls.Add(names); ... }` — stores the CALLER's instance. If test code later mutates the original list (`fileNames.Add("extra")`), the recorded call-history retroactively changes. Fix: `UpgradeBatchCalls.Add(ImmutableArray.CreateRange(names))` (or `[..names]` if the storage's static type is `ImmutableArray<string>` so the compiler picks the immutable conversion). **High-signal failures (3 PRs)**: PR 557 `UpgradeBatchStartedEventArgs.FileNames` populated via `.ToList()` — copy of references but still downcast-mutable, fix is `ImmutableArray<string>`; PR 558 `FakeDatabaseService.UpgradeBatchCalls.Add(fileNames)` stores caller's list; PR #4 `collection-cast-before-defensive-copy` (originally separate slug, now folded here). **Acceptable (do NOT flag)**: returns / fields typed as `ImmutableArray<T>` / `ImmutableList<T>` / `FrozenSet<T>` / `FrozenDictionary<K,V>` / `ImmutableSortedSet<T>` directly (not through a read-only interface); returns of `ReadOnlyCollection<T>` from `.AsReadOnly()` calls; private fields returned ONLY by methods within the same class (no consumer to leak to — verify by reading the access modifier AND that no public method returns the field). **Acceptable but suboptimal**: arrays typed as `IReadOnlyList<T>` AND a maintainer comment documenting "callers MUST NOT cast" — code-comment is weaker than type-enforced immutability but defensible when the consumer surface is small and audited. **Audit method**: for every NEW public/internal property / event-args field / test-fake-stored value typed as `IReadOnly*`, view the producer site. Verify the produced value is `ImmutableArray<T>`-typed (or another immutable collection), OR is wrapped in `.AsReadOnly()`, OR is stored via `ImmutableArray.CreateRange(...)`. If the producer stores a `T[]` or `List<T>` and exposes via `IReadOnly*`, the cast-and-mutate leak exists — flag. |  | HIGH |
| interface-event-non-nullable-vs-impl-nullable-mismatch | review-pass-only | {} | For every NEW or modified event declaration in the diff on an `interface` / `abstract class` whose nullable-reference-type annotation does NOT match the concrete implementation's annotation: verify both sides agree. C# nullable-reference-types (`#nullable enable`) treat `event Action StateChanged;` (non-nullable) and `event Action? StateChanged;` (nullable) as DIFFERENT signatures from a nullable-flow perspective. In a project with `<TreatWarningsAsErrors>true</TreatWarningsAsErrors>` AND `<Nullable>enable</Nullable>`, an interface declaring `event Action StateChanged;` while the implementing class declares `event Action? StateChanged;` produces CS8767-family nullable-mismatch warnings → build fails. **Precondition**: this rule fires ONLY when the project uses `Nullable=enable` AND `TreatWarningsAsErrors=true` (the default for most modern .NET 8+ libraries). Projects with `Nullable=disable` or WaaE off may not surface the diagnostic until later. **High-signal failure (cited from PR #553 round 1)**: `IAnnouncementService.StateChanged` declared as non-nullable `event Action StateChanged;`. Implementation `AnnouncementService` uses `event Action? StateChanged;` (the de facto convention since events have no subscribers by default). Mismatch produced WaaE build failure on PR queue. **Fix patterns**: (a) declare both nullable (`event Action? StateChanged;` on interface AND implementation) — this is the dominant convention because events START null until first subscriber adds; (b) declare both non-nullable AND ensure the implementation initializes with a no-op handler (`event Action StateChanged = static () => { };`); (c) use `EventHandler` / `EventHandler<T>` delegate types — those are nullable by convention. **Acceptable (do NOT flag)**: event declarations in projects with `Nullable=disable` (no warning will fire); event declarations whose interface AND implementation already agree. **Audit method**: for every NEW `event` declaration in an `interface` or `abstract class` modifier in the diff, grep the codebase for implementing classes (`grep -r "class \w*\s*:\s*IXxx"` then look for `event` declarations of the same name); verify the nullable annotation matches. If interface is non-nullable AND implementation is nullable (or vice versa), flag. |  | HIGH |
| razor-empty-css-selector-foiled-by-whitespace-text-nodes | review-pass-only | {} | For every NEW or modified `.razor.css` / `.css` file in the diff that uses the `:empty` pseudo-class selector (e.g., `.toolbar-leading:empty { display: none; }` or `.filter-error:empty { display: none; }`): verify the corresponding markup in the `.razor` file does NOT render any whitespace text node inside the targeted element. `:empty` matches ONLY elements with NO children — and a single newline / indent between tags COUNTS as a whitespace text node in the DOM, defeating `:empty`. **High-signal failure (cited from PR #544 — 2 instances)**: `DatabaseToolsLogView.razor.css:9` has `.toolbar-leading:empty { display: none; }` but the Razor markup renders `<div class="toolbar-leading">\n    @ToolbarLeadingContent\n</div>` — the newline + indent between the opening tag and the `@ToolbarLeadingContent` directive becomes a text node even when the RenderFragment is null. The selector never matches and the empty slot remains visible. Second instance: `DatabaseToolsModal.razor.css` `.filter-error:empty { display: none; }` with markup `<span class="filter-error">\n    @ErrorMessage\n</span>`. **Fix patterns**: (a) render the directive on the SAME line as the element tags: `<div class="toolbar-leading">@ToolbarLeadingContent</div>` (no whitespace between tags); (b) use a conditional in the Razor to omit the element when empty: `@if (ToolbarLeadingContent is not null) { <div class="toolbar-leading">@ToolbarLeadingContent</div> }`; (c) switch CSS to use `display: none` by default and `display: block` only when the slot has a marker class added by the Razor when content is present (`@if (HasContent) { <div class="toolbar-leading has-content"> ... }`); (d) avoid `:empty` entirely — use an `@if`-controlled CSS class. **Acceptable (do NOT flag)**: `:empty` selectors on elements whose markup is GUARANTEED to be tag-only (e.g., `<input>` self-closing tags, or markup `<div></div>` with no whitespace); `:empty` selectors used as a fallback paired with explicit JS / Razor logic that strips whitespace. **Audit method**: regex-grep `:empty\s*\{` in NEW or modified `.razor.css` / `.css` files. For each match, identify the targeted class/selector and grep the corresponding `.razor` for the markup using that class. If the markup has ANY whitespace (newline, indent, space) between the element's opening tag and its content directive, flag. |  | HIGH |
| regex-instance-without-matchtimeout-allows-catastrophic-backtracking | review-pass-only | {} | For every NEW `new Regex(...)` constructor call in the diff, OR for every method that accepts a `Regex` parameter from a caller, verify the regex was constructed with a finite `MatchTimeout` (e.g., `new Regex(pattern, RegexOptions.None, TimeSpan.FromSeconds(1))`). A `Regex` constructed without a timeout has `MatchTimeout = Regex.InfiniteMatchTimeout` — pathological inputs against patterns with nested quantifiers (e.g., `(a+)+$`) cause catastrophic backtracking that hangs the thread indefinitely (CPU pegged, no exception, no timeout). **High-signal failure (cited from PR #544 — 2 instances)**: `ShowProvidersOperation` and `CreateDatabaseOperation` both accept a `Regex` parameter from the request DTO; the operations wrap pattern execution in `try { match.Success } catch (RegexMatchTimeoutException) { ... }` — but `RegexMatchTimeoutException` ONLY fires when the Regex was constructed with a finite `MatchTimeout`. Callers can pass a `Regex` with infinite timeout and the catch never fires. **Fix patterns**: (a) construct regex with timeout at the site: `new Regex(pattern, RegexOptions.None, TimeSpan.FromSeconds(1))`; (b) for methods accepting `Regex` from callers, REJECT regex instances with `regex.MatchTimeout == Regex.InfiniteMatchTimeout` at the entry point (`ArgumentException.ThrowIf(regex.MatchTimeout == Regex.InfiniteMatchTimeout, nameof(regex))`); (c) better, accept the pattern STRING and construct internally with a known-safe timeout; (d) use `[GeneratedRegex(pattern, RegexOptions.None, matchTimeoutMilliseconds: 1000)]` source generator (compile-time guaranteed timeout). **Note**: this rule is `review-pass-only` (not rg-scoped) because `new Regex(...)` calls commonly span multiple lines (multi-line argument formatting), which line-based rg cannot reliably parse. A reviewer must read the full constructor argument list to determine whether the third (MatchTimeout) arg is present. **Acceptable (do NOT flag)**: `Regex` constructed with the `[GeneratedRegex(..., matchTimeoutMilliseconds: ...)]` source-generator attribute; `Regex` instances stored in static field with an explicit timeout (`private static readonly Regex Pattern = new(..., TimeSpan.FromMilliseconds(500))`); test code constructing regex against known-safe patterns; consumed-internally regex where the pattern + input are both compile-time constants. **Audit method**: review every NEW `new Regex(` site in the diff (multi-line aware) — check that the constructor includes a `TimeSpan` argument. Review every NEW method signature accepting `Regex` and verify the entry point rejects infinite-timeout instances. |  | HIGH |
| namespace-mismatch-symbol-referenced-without-using | review-pass-only | {} | For every NEW symbol reference in the diff (a type name, an extension method call, an `@using` directive in Razor) where the file's namespace cannot reach the symbol's declaring namespace through C# simple-name lookup OR explicit `using` directive OR `_Imports.razor`: verify the symbol's namespace is reachable. C# `dotnet build` catches most cases via CS0246 / CS0103 — this rule still applies for THREE surfaces where the failure is NOT compile-time-fast: (a) **Razor `@using` omissions in `.razor` files** where the type is only referenced in markup — the error surfaces at Razor compile time (slower CI roundtrip) or runtime; (b) **doc-comment `<see cref="..."/>` references** where invalid cref produces a warning the IDE doesn't always surface immediately AND that becomes an error under WaaE; (c) **conditional-compilation guards** where the `using` is inside `#if DEBUG` but the type usage is unconditional — the missing-using surfaces only in Release builds. **C# lookup is hierarchical (per ECMA-334 §13.8)**: simple-name lookup walks the enclosing namespaces from innermost to outermost, so a type declared in a PARENT namespace IS visible from any DESCENDANT namespace WITHOUT a `using` directive. The opposite is NOT true — a parent namespace cannot reach a CHILD namespace's symbols without an explicit `using`. See `FP-1: parent-namespace-not-in-scope` for the canonical false-positive pattern this rule MUST avoid. **High-signal failure (Razor sub-case)**: `Foo.Bar.Views.Page.razor` markup references `<MyComponent />` where `MyComponent` lives in a SIBLING namespace `Foo.Bar.Components` — no `@using Foo.Bar.Components` in `_Imports.razor` or in the file. The C# compiler catches `using`-omissions in `.razor.cs`, but markup-only references go through the Razor compile pipeline, where the error message is less direct and may not surface until first render in dev workflows. **High-signal failure (cref sub-case)**: `<see cref="ICriticalAbstraction.Method"/>` doc-comment in a file whose namespace doesn't reach `ICriticalAbstraction` — under `<GenerateDocumentationFile>true</GenerateDocumentationFile>` + WaaE the missing-symbol becomes a build error that's only emitted when docs are produced (Release / publish builds). **Fix patterns**: (a) add the explicit `using` at the top of the file (for `.cs`); (b) add `@using Sibling.Namespace` to `_Imports.razor` (for Razor); (c) fully-qualify the reference (`global::Foo.Bar.Components.MyComponent`) when an explicit `using` would conflict; (d) move the symbol to a `GlobalUsings.cs` if used pervasively. **Acceptable (do NOT flag — these are valid C# OR caught by FP-1)**: symbols in a PARENT namespace of the file's namespace (per ECMA-334 §13.8 — see FP-1); symbols imported via explicit `using`; symbols imported via project-level `<Using Include="..."/>` MSBuild items or `GlobalUsings.cs`; framework types (`System.*`, `Microsoft.*`) where `<ImplicitUsings>enable</ImplicitUsings>` is on; cases where the C# build would catch a real CS0246/CS0103 (rule fires only for the 3 surfaces above where the build doesn't catch fast). **Audit method**: for every NEW Razor markup tag, `@using`, or `<see cref>` reference in the diff, identify the containing namespace of the symbol via grep / IDE lookup. If the symbol is in a SIBLING / unrelated / child namespace (NOT a parent namespace) AND no matching `using` / `_Imports.razor` entry exists, flag. Cross-check against FP-1 before flagging — if the supposed-missing namespace is a parent of the file's namespace, the rule does NOT apply. |  | HIGH |
| facade-discards-inner-result-discriminator | review-pass-only | {} | For every NEW or modified wrapper / facade / adapter method in the diff that wraps a result-discriminator-returning inner call (`Result<T>`, custom `Outcome` enum, `(bool Success, T Value)` tuple, `XxxResult` record with a `WasSomethingHappened` flag): verify the wrapper PROPAGATES the discriminator distinction up the call chain. Wrappers that collapse a multi-valued result to a single bool (`return true` on no-exception, `return false` on exception) silently lose meaningful caller-distinctions. **High-signal failure (cited from PR #546 round 1)**: `MauiMenuActionService.TryOpenModalAsync(...)` calls `await ModalCoordinator.TryOpenAsync(...)` which returns `ModalOpenResult` with a `WasOpened` boolean (false on preemption-veto). The wrapper does `await ModalCoordinator.TryOpenAsync(...); return true;` — ignoring the `WasOpened` field entirely. Callers (e.g., `AttentionBanner.OpenSettingsAsync`) expect `false` when Settings failed to open (veto case) so they can show an error. With `return true` always, the error path silently never fires. **Fix patterns**: (a) propagate the discriminator: `var result = await ModalCoordinator.TryOpenAsync(...); return result.WasOpened;`; (b) return the inner result type unchanged from the wrapper (`Task<ModalOpenResult>` not `Task<bool>`); (c) if the wrapper's `bool` return has DIFFERENT semantics from the inner discriminator, document the mapping explicitly in XML doc AND verify the mapping is correct for every inner discriminator value. **Acceptable (do NOT flag)**: wrappers that genuinely don't care about the inner discriminator (e.g., a fire-and-forget kick-off where caller never branches on the outcome — verify by reading caller code); wrappers where the inner result has only ONE distinguishable outcome (no discriminator to lose); wrappers whose `bool` return is documented to mean "called successfully without exception" rather than "operation achieved its goal". **Audit method**: for every NEW wrapper-style method in the diff (signature pattern: takes args, calls one or two inner methods, returns `bool` or `Task<bool>`), view the inner method's return type. If the inner return type has named fields / properties / discriminator values BEYOND success/failure, the wrapper's collapse to `bool` likely loses information. Cross-check by viewing the wrapper's CALLER sites for branches on the bool return — if a caller's `if (await wrapper.X()) { /* A */ } else { /* B */ }` would benefit from a third outcome, the wrapper is too lossy. |  | HIGH |
| record-non-nullable-ctor-validation | review-pass-only | {} | For every NEW `record` / `record struct` / `class` with a primary constructor declaration in the diff that has one or more non-nullable reference-type parameters (`string`, `IReadOnlyList<T>`, custom class): verify the constructor body OR a `[Required]`-equivalent attribute validates that the caller did not pass `null` via the `null!` escape hatch or `default(T)` cast. C# nullable annotations are compile-time hints — at runtime, callers can still pass `null` via `null!`, reflection, deserialization, or `default(T)`. A non-nullable parameter on a record's primary constructor is part of the public contract; without a runtime null check, downstream `NullReferenceException`s surface far from the construction site. **High-signal failure (cited from consuming-pr-3)**: `public sealed record UserSession(string Id, IReadOnlyList<Permission> Permissions);` — caller does `new UserSession(id, null!)` and the record stores `null` in `Permissions`. Later, `session.Permissions.Count` throws NRE in some unrelated method. **Fix patterns**: (a) explicit null check in primary constructor body: `public sealed record UserSession(string Id, IReadOnlyList<Permission> Permissions) { public string Id { get; } = Id ?? throw new ArgumentNullException(nameof(Id)); public IReadOnlyList<Permission> Permissions { get; } = Permissions ?? throw new ArgumentNullException(nameof(Permissions)); }`; (b) use `ArgumentNullException.ThrowIfNull(Id); ArgumentNullException.ThrowIfNull(Permissions);` in the primary constructor body (records support this); (c) use System.Diagnostics.CodeAnalysis `[NotNull]` attribute pairing where applicable. **Acceptable (do NOT flag)**: records with all-nullable reference params; records explicitly documented as "framework-deserialization only, post-construction validation occurs in the deserializer wrapper"; primitive struct params (value types can't be null); records whose construction sites are 100% within the same assembly AND audited to never pass `null!`. **Audit method**: for every NEW `record` / primary-constructor `class` declaration in the diff, list each parameter's nullability. For each non-nullable reference-type parameter, verify the constructor body or property initializer includes a null check. If absent, flag. |  | HIGH |
| state-read-stale-after-event-publication | review-pass-only | {} | For every NEW pattern in the diff where (a) one method PUBLISHES an event / raises a notification / completes a TCS / dispatches to a Fluxor action that has a corresponding event handler / subscriber doing reactive state-clear / pull-from-store work, AND (b) ANOTHER method or call site READS the same state via a getter / property / `Get*` method between the publish-event and the subscriber's reactive-clear completing: verify the reader's contract allows observing the PRE-clear state, OR the reader is forced to wait for the subscriber to converge. State-reads in the window between event-publish and reactive-clear return STALE state — and the reader may not realize it's racing. **High-signal failure (cited from consuming-pr-3)**: `ServiceRegistry.UnregisterAsync(id)` raises `RegistrationRemoved` event and returns. A Fluxor effect subscribes to that event and does `state.Registrations[id] = null` reactively — but `ServiceRegistry.GetRegistration(id)` (a synchronous lookup) still returns the old registration for the duration of the effect's dispatch. Callers calling `Get` right after `Unregister` see stale data. **Fix patterns**: (a) make the publisher do the state-clear inline (synchronous) BEFORE raising the event — readers immediately see clean state; (b) make the reader async + wait for the subscriber's completion via TCS; (c) document the eventual-consistency window explicitly in XML doc on the reader AND ensure no caller depends on immediate consistency; (d) include the post-clear state in the event args so consumers don't need to re-read. **Acceptable (do NOT flag)**: read paths explicitly documented as eventually-consistent; reads on a different observable (the cleared state is on store A; the read is from store B that's not yet affected); patterns where the publish-then-read interleaving is forbidden by an outer lock. **Audit method**: for every NEW event-raise / TCS-complete / Fluxor `Dispatch(action)` in the diff, identify the subscriber(s). If a subscriber mutates state observable via a synchronous getter elsewhere in the codebase, flag the read sites that could race. |  | HIGH |

## Patterns (lower-frequency, 3-4 hits in seed corpus)

| js-interop-lifecycle | diff-scoped | {"pattern":"JSRuntime\\.InvokeAsync<IJSObjectReference>\|catch \\(JSException\|catch \\(JSDisconnectedException","glob":["*.cs","*.razor.cs"]} |  |  |
