| perf-batching | review-pass-only | {} | For every operation/component in the diff that emits per-row, per-entry, or per-iteration UI updates (`StateHasChanged`, `Progress<T>.Report`, `InvokeAsync(StateHasChanged)`, event-handler chains): verify the update is batched/throttled — not called on every loop iteration that may run thousands of times. For every data-source enumeration (provider list, log entries, EF Core query results): verify single-pass — flag patterns like "load names first, then load details in a second pass" that imply N+1 or double-scan. For every async-foreach loop: verify the loop body doesn't await per-item if the workload is independent (consider `Task.WhenAll` + batching). |  |
| logic-inversion | review-pass-only | {} | For every early-return / short-circuit guard in modified `.cs`/`.razor.cs` files with predicates involving `!=`, `!`, `!=!`, or double-negatives: re-read the guard and verify it exits when state is the desired state, not when state is the OPPOSITE. Common bug: `if (_show != !isPinned) return;` — when `_show == !isPinned` (desired-state-already-set), this DOES exit; otherwise it doesn't. Verify the polarity matches intent. Ternary expressions returning the wrong branch (`condition ? badValue : goodValue`) and cross-method state flips where state is inverted twice and end up wrong are also caught here. |  |
| iasyncdisposable-fp | review-pass-only | {} | For every `await using` over a BCL type: verify the type implements `IAsyncDisposable` per Microsoft docs. Common FP: `ServiceProvider` (does implement both since .NET Core 3.0); `Stream` (does since .NET Core 3.0). Confirmed IDisposable-only: `SemaphoreSlim`, `HttpClient` (per .NET 8 docs). Cross-reference fp-2 below. | fp-2 |
| off-by-one-bounds | review-pass-only | {} | For every NEW loop / index expression / range slice / `<= length` vs `< length` comparison / `array[length - N]` indexing / `Span.Slice(start, length)` added in the diff: verify the bounds are correct against the underlying collection size. **High-signal failures**: `for (int i = 0; i <= array.Length; i++)` — off by one (overruns); `array[array.Length - 1]` on an empty array — out of bounds; `text.Substring(start, length)` where `start + length > text.Length`; `Span.Slice(offset, count)` where `offset + count` overflows the span; pagination math `skip = (page - 1) * pageSize` with `page = 0`; `list.RemoveAt(0)` in a tight loop without re-checking `list.Count`. **Acceptable (do NOT flag)**: bounds where the loop-invariant proves correctness AND the proof is documented in a one-line comment; pre-checked emptiness (`if (array.Length == 0) return; ... array[0]`); BCL methods that are documented to throw rather than corrupt (`ImmutableArray.Builder.Add` cannot overflow). Use the diff to find NEW bounds-sensitive operations; pre-existing patterns are not in scope. |  |
| input-validation-missing | review-pass-only | {} | For every NEW public method / public API endpoint / `[Parameter]` setter / `[HttpPost]` / `[HttpPut]` handler / event handler that takes untrusted input (`string`, byte[], collection, deserialized DTO) added in the diff: verify the input is validated at the entry point against length, format, range, allowed-value set, or other domain invariants. **High-signal failures**: HTTP endpoint accepts `string note` with no `[StringLength]` / `[MaxLength]` — DoS via gigabyte payload; method takes `int count` without `count > 0` check, leading to negative-loop math; takes `DateTime when` with no validation, allowing `DateTime.MinValue` propagation; takes `string filePath` consumed via `File.ReadAllBytes` with no validation. **Required defense**: `ArgumentException.ThrowIfNullOrEmpty(name)`, `ArgumentOutOfRangeException.ThrowIfNegativeOrZero(count)`, `[Required] [StringLength(...)]` attributes, custom domain validator at the entry point. **Acceptable (do NOT flag)**: internal methods consumed only by validated callers (trace the lineage); strongly-typed-id wrapper types whose constructor already validated (cross-reference `strongly-typed-id-preference`); inputs already validated by middleware (FluentValidation pipeline, model-binding [Required]). Use the diff to find NEW entry points; pre-existing unvalidated APIs in baseline are not in scope. |  |
| xss-injection-risk | review-pass-only | {} | For every NEW user-controlled value rendered into HTML / JavaScript / SQL / shell context added in the diff: verify proper encoding/escaping/parameterization. **High-signal failures**: Razor `@((MarkupString)userInput)` — bypasses Blazor\u0027s default HTML encoding; `string sql = $"SELECT * FROM users WHERE name = \u0027{name}\u0027";` — SQL injection via concatenation; `Process.Start("cmd.exe", $"/c echo {userInput}")` — command injection; `Response.WriteAsync($"<div>{userMessage}</div>")` — XSS via direct write without `HtmlEncoder`. **Required defenses**: `HtmlEncoder.Default.Encode(value)`, parameterized SQL (`SqlCommand.Parameters.Add`), shell-argument escaping (`Process.Start(new ProcessStartInfo { FileName = "cmd.exe", ArgumentList = { ... } })`), Blazor\u0027s default `@expression` encoding (do NOT use `@((MarkupString)...)` unless the source is already encoded). **Acceptable (do NOT flag)**: values from compile-time constants; values from an already-validated allow-list; values rendered into a context that explicitly tolerates the format (`<script>` JSON-stringified via `JsonSerializer.Serialize` — Blazor already handles); values run through a documented sanitization library. Use the diff to find NEW user-controlled render sites; pre-existing patterns in baseline are not in scope. |  |
| shell-script-discipline | diff-scoped | {"pattern":"^#!/(usr/)?bin/(ba)?sh","glob":["**/*.sh","**/.devcontainer/setup*"]} |  |  |
| code-duplication-dry | review-pass-only | {} | For every NEW block of code added in the diff (>=5 lines): scan the diff and the surrounding files (within the same project / src tree) for substantively-identical blocks. **High-signal patterns**: same parameter assignment repeated across N method overloads (`SetA(...) { /* boilerplate */ Foo(x); } SetB(...) { /* same boilerplate */ Foo(y); }` — factor out the boilerplate); same try/catch/log block repeated across three different services; **same try/catch/exception-routing shape repeated across N≥2 methods OF THE SAME NEW FILE** (e.g., `DispatchSafelyAsync(Action)` and `OpenRecoveryDialogAsync(Func<Task>)` both wrap an outer `try { await dispatch(() => { try { work } catch { route } }); } catch { route }` — extract a shared inner-catch helper `SafeInvoke(Action)` / `SafeInvokeAsync(Func<Task>)` so the outer dispatch only owns the dispatch-level catch); same validation-then-throw sequence in three constructors; same DTO mapping repeated across read/write methods. Flag the duplication and propose: (a) shared private helper, (b) base-class template method, (c) generic parameterization, (d) source-generator if the pattern is highly mechanical. **Acceptable (do NOT flag)**: duplication that aids isolation (e.g., test fixtures intentionally repeated to avoid cross-test coupling); duplication that\u0027s likely to diverge soon (single point of variation pending); duplication across security-boundary or assembly-boundary where coupling them would be worse; 5-line "duplications" that are actually just identical boilerplate signatures (`public override void Dispose() => ...`) without shared logic. **REPEAT-MISS DISCIPLINE (2x in lightweight-gate-v5 history — PR 4 `TryOpenModalAsync` helper + PR 5 `OpenRecoveryDialogAsync`)**: when a NEW file contains N≥2 methods with try/catch shapes, panel slots MUST explicitly enumerate them by name and compare shapes before declaring READY. Cross-reference: §2C `DRY remediation gate` covers commit-time DRY audit; this rule covers in-PR-review-time DRY observation. Use the diff to find NEW blocks; baseline duplications are out of scope (separate refactor work). |  |
| ui-verb-terminology-inconsistency-across-surfaces | review-pass-only | {} | For every NEW or modified user-facing action verb in the diff that names the SAME logical operation across multiple UI surfaces (e.g., a button label + a context-menu item + a confirmation-dialog title + a toast message + an aria-label all referring to the same "remove this database" action): verify the verb is consistent across all surfaces. Mixing verbs ("Delete" on the button vs "Remove" in the context menu vs "Discard" in the confirmation) confuses users who don't realize the three labels describe the same action. **High-signal failure (cited from PR #558)**: `ManageDatabasesTab.razor:111` bulk action button reads "Delete N" while the row context-menu item reads "Remove" and the confirmation dialog says "Are you sure you want to remove {fileName}?". Three verbs for one action. **Fix patterns**: (a) pick ONE verb per action (project-level convention is usually "Remove" for non-destructive UI removal, "Delete" for destructive); (b) audit ALL surfaces in the diff that name the action — button label, menu item, confirmation title, confirmation body, toast/banner success message, toast/banner error message, aria-label, telemetry event name — and confirm they all use the same verb; (c) extract to a constants file (`UiActionLabels.Remove`) if the same string appears 3+ times. **Acceptable (do NOT flag)**: cases where two surfaces describe different aspects of the action (e.g., "Remove" for the removal step + "Cleanup" for the post-removal disposal — different operations); UI that follows a documented project convention deviating from this rule (cite the convention in the PR description). **Audit method**: for any NEW user-action UI element in the diff, search the diff for OTHER references to the same action by data-flow (same `@onclick` handler, same `EventCallback`, same Razor `case ActionKind.X:`). Compare the verb across each reference; flag inconsistencies. |  | MEDIUM |
| prefers-reduced-motion-not-extended-to-new-transition | review-pass-only | {} | For every NEW CSS rule in the diff that adds an `animation` / `transition` / `transform` property (any non-`none` value) to an element class: verify the SAME stylesheet's existing `@media (prefers-reduced-motion: reduce)` override block (if one exists) ALSO lists the new class. Otherwise, users with reduced-motion accessibility preference will see the new animation in violation of their stated preference (WCAG 2.1 SC 2.3.3 Animation from Interactions). **High-signal failure (cited from PR #558 round 6)**: `DatabaseEntryRow.razor.css:155` added a `transition: max-width 0.2s, padding-right 0.2s` to `.db-entry-checkbox`. The existing reduced-motion block in the same file (`@media (prefers-reduced-motion: reduce) { .db-entry-row, .db-entry-upgrading, .db-entry-upgrading-text, .db-entry-cancel-btn { animation: none; transition: none; } }`) did NOT include `.db-entry-checkbox`. Bot flagged twice across 2 rounds; user with reduced-motion preference still sees the slide-in animation. **Fix patterns**: (a) add the new class to the existing reduced-motion selector list: `.db-entry-row, .db-entry-upgrading, ..., .db-entry-checkbox { animation: none; transition: none; }`; (b) use a wildcard / attribute-based override at the top-level (`@media (prefers-reduced-motion: reduce) { *, *::before, *::after { animation-duration: 0.01ms !important; transition-duration: 0.01ms !important; } }` — opinionated but covers everything); (c) extract a CSS custom property for transition duration and override the variable in the reduced-motion block. **Acceptable (do NOT flag)**: stylesheets without an existing `prefers-reduced-motion` block (no precedent to extend; the original file's accessibility-strategy choice was no-block — DON'T introduce one unilaterally); transitions on properties that don't cause perceptible motion (color-only transitions, opacity-fade-only); animations whose duration is already <100ms (below human perception threshold for vestibular response). **Audit method**: for every NEW CSS rule with `animation:` / `transition:` / `transform:` in modified `.css` / `.razor.css` files, grep the same file for `@media (prefers-reduced-motion: reduce)`. If found, verify the targeted class/selector appears in the reduced-motion override; if absent, flag. |  | MEDIUM |
| extension-method-style-inconsistency-with-codebase | review-pass-only | {} | For every NEW extension method declaration in the diff (`public static class Xyz` containing `public static <Type> Method(this <T> source, ...)` OR the newer C# 14 `extension(<T> source) { public <Type> Method(...) { } }` block syntax): verify the declaration style matches the CONVENTION used by sibling extension methods in the same project / namespace. Mixing the two syntaxes within a codebase creates visual noise + cognitive overhead for readers + complicates IDE refactoring. **High-signal failure (cited from PR #558)**: `MenuHostDependenciesExtensions.cs` + `DatabaseToolsTestDependencies.cs` use the new C# 14 `extension(IServiceCollection services) { public void AddXxx(...) { ... } }` block syntax, but every other extension method in the same test project (`BannerHostDependenciesExtensions`, `ModalServiceDependenciesExtensions`, etc.) uses the traditional `public static class X { public static IServiceCollection AddXxx(this IServiceCollection services, ...) { ... } }` form. Bot flagged twice on consistency. **Fix patterns**: (a) use the traditional `public static class X { public static ... this T ... }` syntax to match existing siblings; (b) IF the project explicitly chose to migrate to C# 14 extension blocks, document the convention in `README.md` AND open follow-up PRs to convert siblings — DON'T leave the codebase in a half-migrated state; (c) ask via `ask_user` which convention the project is adopting before writing the first new extension method. **Acceptable (do NOT flag)**: net-new projects with no sibling extension methods (style is a fresh decision); projects whose `README.md` documents an in-flight migration; first extension method introduced into a project where the language version was just bumped to support the new syntax (with an `ask_user` confirmation). **Audit method**: for every NEW extension-method declaration in the diff, grep the same project / namespace for OTHER `(this \w+\s+\w+)` patterns (traditional) AND `extension\(\w+\s+\w+\)` patterns (C# 14). If both exist, the project is in a mixed state — flag. |  | MEDIUM |
| empty-collection-default-comparer-mismatch | review-pass-only | {} | For every NEW property / parameter / field of type `FrozenSet<TKey>` / `FrozenDictionary<TKey, TValue>` / `HashSet<TKey>` / `Dictionary<TKey, TValue>` / `ImmutableHashSet<TKey>` / `ImmutableSortedDictionary<TKey, TValue>` in the diff that uses a default empty-collection value (e.g., `FrozenSet<string>.Empty`, `ImmutableHashSet<string>.Empty`): verify the default's comparer matches the comparer used by consumers that read into the collection. The static `.Empty` instances are created with the type's DEFAULT comparer — for `FrozenSet<string>.Empty` that's `EqualityComparer<string>.Default` (case-sensitive `Ordinal` comparison). If consumers do case-insensitive lookups via `OrdinalIgnoreCase` (common pattern for file names, header names, identifier names), the empty default is INCOMPATIBLE — silent semantic mismatch that surfaces only when callers add elements with one comparer and read them with another. **High-signal failure (cited from PR #557)**: `BannerProgressEntry.cs:23` declared `public required FrozenSet<string> BatchFileNames { get; init; } = FrozenSet<string>.Empty;` while the rest of the codebase looks up file names case-insensitively via `OrdinalIgnoreCase`. Default-empty instances would behave inconsistently with non-empty instances created via `fileNames.ToFrozenSet(StringComparer.OrdinalIgnoreCase)`. **Fix patterns**: (a) use a domain-specific static field defaulting with the right comparer: `private static readonly FrozenSet<string> EmptyOrdinalIgnoreCase = FrozenSet.ToFrozenSet(Array.Empty<string>(), StringComparer.OrdinalIgnoreCase); public required FrozenSet<string> BatchFileNames { get; init; } = EmptyOrdinalIgnoreCase;`; (b) require callers to always populate the collection (drop the default, use `required`); (c) for `ImmutableHashSet`/`ImmutableSortedSet`, use `ImmutableHashSet.Create<string>(StringComparer.OrdinalIgnoreCase)` as the default. **Acceptable (do NOT flag)**: collections whose consumers genuinely use default comparison (case-sensitive `Ordinal` for unique-id strings, byte-sequence content, etc.); collections where the type is `IReadOnlyCollection<T>` / `IEnumerable<T>` (no comparer surface); collections that are guaranteed-empty-and-never-read (e.g., a stub used only as a sentinel). **Audit method**: for every NEW collection property / parameter typed as `FrozenSet<T>` / `FrozenDictionary` / `HashSet` / `Dictionary` / `ImmutableHashSet` / `ImmutableSortedDictionary` with a default-empty value, grep for adjacent code that POPULATES the same collection type via `.ToFrozenSet(StringComparer.X)` or similar. If the populated path specifies a non-default comparer, the default-empty path is mismatched. |  | MEDIUM |
| registry-stack-active-change-no-state-replay | review-pass-only | {} | For every NEW or modified stack-based observer registry / multi-host coordinator in the diff (registries that track an "active" item from a stack of registered items, e.g., `IMenuHostRegistry`, `IModalCoordinator` with a host stack, multi-instance singleton routers): verify the moment the ACTIVE item changes (push, pop, swap), the newly-active item receives a state-snapshot / catch-up event covering the state that was already active when it became the new active. Without this, the newly-active observer enters a "stale" state where it doesn't know about pre-existing condition (e.g., a menu was already open when this host became the topmost; the host renders no menu because it never got the open-state event). **High-signal failure (cited from PR #558 round 1)**: `MenuHost.razor.cs:115` — when `MenuService.ActiveItems != null` (a menu IS open) and the active `MenuHost` changes via `MenuHostRegistry.SetActive(newHost)`, the newly-active host never receives a `MenuService.StateChanged` event. `_focusedMenuId` remains stale; the new host renders an empty menu area while a menu is conceptually open. **Fix patterns**: (a) on registry `SetActive(newHost)`, immediately invoke a state-replay callback on `newHost` (`newHost.OnStateRehydrate(currentSnapshot)`); (b) subscribe the registry to the underlying state-source and re-broadcast on every active-change AS WELL as state-change; (c) have the newly-active item proactively pull state on its `OnActivated` lifecycle hook (pull-based rehydrate); (d) document the eventual-consistency window if state-replay is genuinely too expensive. **Acceptable (do NOT flag)**: registries whose state is genuinely stateless (no relevant cross-active state to replay); registries where active-change is RARE and a single missed initial render is acceptable; registries with a single active item per session lifetime (no replay scenario). **Audit method**: for every NEW registry / coordinator class in the diff that has a `SetActive`/`Register`/`Push`-style method AND an associated state-source, view the `SetActive` body. If it does NOT invoke a state-replay on the new active item, flag. |  | MEDIUM |
| repeated-collection-scan-in-click-handler | review-pass-only | {} | For every NEW or modified UI click handler / event handler / command handler in the diff that operates on a user-selection set: verify the handler does NOT scan the underlying source collection (`Entries`, `Items`, `_dataSource`) multiple times via `FirstOrDefault` / `Where` / repeated `foreach` loops AND additionally call helpers that re-scan internally. Repeated scans turn an O(N) operation into O(N²) or worse for large selection counts; user-perceived UI latency on bulk operations. **High-signal failure (cited from PR #558)**: `ManageDatabasesTab.razor.cs:762` `OnBulkUpgradeClickAsync` does `foreach (var selected in _selectedForRemoval) { var entry = DatabaseService.Entries.FirstOrDefault(e => e.FileName == selected); ... }` AND `IsUpgradeEligibleForSelected()` (called separately) ALSO scans `DatabaseService.Entries`. With 200 databases selected, that's 200 * 200 = 40000 comparisons just for the eligibility check. **Fix patterns**: (a) build a `Dictionary<string, DatabaseEntry>` snapshot ONCE at handler start, then look up each selection by O(1) key access; (b) use `Lookup<string, DatabaseEntry>` if multiple entries can share a key; (c) pre-filter the source collection ONCE into a smaller collection of selected entries, then operate on that; (d) push the eligibility check into the source so it runs against an indexed view. **Acceptable (do NOT flag)**: handlers operating on small fixed-size selections (≤ 5 items by design); handlers where the scan happens once + cached at handler start; handlers where the source collection itself is already a `Dictionary` / `Lookup` / indexed view. **Audit method**: for every NEW click/event handler in the diff, count the distinct `.FirstOrDefault(`, `.Single(`, `.Where(`, `foreach (var x in <sourceCollection>)` invocations against the SAME source. If ≥2 against the same source, AND the source's expected size is unbounded user data, flag with O(N²) warning. |  | MEDIUM |
| scope-state-not-reset-on-tab-restart-or-rerun | review-pass-only | {} | For every NEW or modified component / service / tab / workflow in the diff that supports a "restart" / "re-run" / "reset" / "new-session" lifecycle hook AND tracks per-session state in instance fields (counters, `_lastRenderedCount`, `_processedIds`, `_seenFiles`, sticky flags): verify EVERY tracked state field is reset to its initial value on the lifecycle restart, OR is explicitly documented as cross-session-cumulative. Forgotten fields silently carry stale state across runs — surfacing as "the second run doesn't render entries past the first run's count", "the second import sees duplicates from the first run's set", "the cancel button stays disabled after the first run completes". **High-signal failure (cited from PR #544)**: `DatabaseToolsLogView.razor.cs:189` — `_lastRenderedCount` is only updated when `Entries.Count` increases. When the tab starts a NEW run, it resets `Entries` to empty but `_lastRenderedCount` remains at the previous run's count. Subsequent entries (indices 1..N) never satisfy `Entries.Count > _lastRenderedCount` (because N < previous count) — auto-scroll never fires for the new run. **Fix patterns**: (a) reset every per-session state field in the restart method: `private void StartNewRun() { Entries = []; _lastRenderedCount = 0; _processedIds.Clear(); _hasErrored = false; ... }`; (b) extract per-session state into a `SessionState` record that gets re-created on restart (impossible to forget a field); (c) introduce a `[SessionScoped]` marker attribute + reflection-based reset (overkill for most cases but works for very large state surfaces). **Acceptable (do NOT flag)**: state that's genuinely cumulative across runs (total-since-startup counters — document with `_cumulativeTotal` naming); state whose reset is handled by re-creating the entire component instance (parent re-renders the child); state explicitly tied to the parent / outer lifetime. **Audit method**: for every NEW or modified instance field in a "restartable" component / service, grep the same class for the restart/reset method. If the field is NOT assigned in the restart method AND is NOT documented as cumulative, flag. |  | MEDIUM |
| js-interop-module-attach-without-detach-lifecycle | review-pass-only | {} | For every NEW JS module / IJSObjectReference in the diff that registers a listener / observer / interval / DOM-event-handler / subscription in an `attach()` / `init()` / `register()` function: verify the module ALSO exports a corresponding `detach()` / `dispose()` / `unregister()` function AND that the corresponding `.razor.cs` or consumer calls it from `DisposeAsync` / `Dispose`. Listeners registered without detach LEAK on component disposal — the JS event handler holds a reference to the .NET callback (`DotNetObjectReference`), which keeps the .NET component alive past intended disposal, leading to "method invoked on disposed instance" exceptions in the JS handler. **High-signal failure (cited from PR #544)**: `DatabaseToolsLogView.js:18` exports `attach()` which calls `addEventListener('scroll', handler)` but exports no `detach()`. The corresponding `DatabaseToolsLogView.razor.cs.DisposeAsync` has no way to remove the listener. Each modal open/close leaks a scroll-handler holding a `DotNetObjectReference`. **Fix patterns**: (a) export a paired `detach()` function: `export function attach(...) { ... ; return () => detachHandler(); }` returning a cleanup callback; (b) export named `detach()`: `export function detach() { element.removeEventListener('scroll', handler); }`; (c) wire detach into the component's `DisposeAsync`: `await _jsModule.InvokeVoidAsync("detach"); _jsModule.DisposeAsync();`; (d) use `AddEventListener` with `signal: abortController.signal` and abort on disposal (modern web platform — single call cleans up multiple listeners). **Acceptable (do NOT flag)**: JS modules that register listeners on the WINDOW or DOCUMENT (not component-scoped) and are intentionally lifetime-of-page; JS modules whose listeners are stateless and idempotent (re-firing is harmless, no .NET callback held); listeners registered on elements that themselves get garbage-collected when the component unmounts (rare — verify). **Audit method**: for every NEW JS module file in the diff (`.js` / `.razor.js`), grep for `addEventListener\|setInterval\|MutationObserver\|new IntersectionObserver\|new ResizeObserver\|Reflect.has`. For each match, grep the same file for a corresponding `removeEventListener\|clearInterval\|.disconnect()\|.abort()`. If missing, flag. |  | MEDIUM |
| pluralization-singular-plural-grammar-mismatch | review-pass-only | {} | For every NEW user-facing string in the diff that interpolates a count + a noun + a verb form (`$"{count} database needs attention"`, `$"{count} file was imported"`, `$"{n} item is selected"`): verify the verb agrees with the singular-vs-plural form of the count. `count == 1` requires singular verb ("needs", "was", "is", "has"); `count != 1` requires plural verb ("need", "were", "are", "have"). Off-by-one grammar is jarring and reads as machine-generated. **High-signal failure (cited from PR #546)**: `AttentionBanner.razor` renders `$"{AttentionCount} database{(AttentionCount == 1 ? "" : "s")} need attention"` — pluralizes "database/databases" correctly but uses singular-form "need" always. For count == 1: "1 database need attention" — grammatically wrong. **Fix patterns**: (a) explicit conditional on the verb: `$"{count} database{(count == 1 ? "" : "s")} {(count == 1 ? "needs" : "need")} attention"`; (b) use a helper method `Pluralize(count, "database", "databases", "needs", "need")`; (c) restructure to avoid the count + verb pair: `$"{(count == 1 ? "1 database needs" : $"{count} databases need")} attention"`; (d) use a localization library that handles plural rules (ICU MessageFormat, `IStringLocalizer<T>.GetString` with `pluralForm` parameter). **Acceptable (do NOT flag)**: count interpolations with no following verb ("3 results"); strings that ONLY ever fire for count > 1 (verify by inspecting the call site's guard); strings whose verb form is invariant ("count failed: {count}" — no verb agreement needed). **Audit method**: for every NEW string interpolation in the diff containing `\b\d+\b` or `{\w+}` + `(was\|were\|is\|are\|has\|have\|need\|needs\|do\|does)\b`, verify the verb's plural-form is conditional on the count. If the verb is hardcoded to one form, flag. |  | MEDIUM |
| strongly-typed-id-none-rejection | review-pass-only | {} | For every NEW method in the diff that accepts a strongly-typed ID parameter (`UserId`, `EntryId`, `SessionId` etc.) and uses it to look up / match / route to a target: verify the method explicitly rejects the sentinel `None`/`Empty`/`Default(T)` value AT METHOD ENTRY. Strongly-typed IDs have a `None`/`Empty`/`Guid.Empty`-wrapping value to represent "unset" — if a caller passes that value, the lookup matches NOTHING (best case) or MATCHES the wrong thing if the registry was initialized with a default-keyed entry (worst case). **High-signal failure (cited from consuming-pr-3)**: `ServiceRegistry.Register(ServiceId id, IService service)` — caller passes `ServiceId.None` (sentinel from a not-yet-initialized request). Registry accepts the `None` key, the `Find(ServiceId.None)` later returns this service instead of nothing. **Fix patterns**: (a) `ArgumentException.ThrowIf(id == ServiceId.None, nameof(id))` at method entry; (b) make the strongly-typed-id constructor reject `Guid.Empty`/`default(T)` (then `ServiceId.None` doesn't even exist as a valid value); (c) accept `ServiceId?` (nullable) — caller MUST pass a value, ambiguity disappears. **Acceptable (do NOT flag)**: methods that LEGITIMATELY accept `None` as "no filter" / "all entries" semantics (document this in XML doc); IDs whose `None` value is a valid domain value (rare — design smell). **Audit method**: for every NEW method accepting a strongly-typed ID parameter, view the body. If the body uses `id` without first checking `id != ServiceId.None` (or equivalent), flag. |  | MEDIUM |
| init-property-invariant-bypass | review-pass-only | {} | For every NEW or modified `record` / `class` declaration in the diff that has a primary constructor enforcing an invariant (validation, range check, normalization) AND ALSO has `init`-settable properties: verify the `init` properties cannot bypass the invariant via `with`-expression. `record SomeRecord(int Count) { public required int Count { get; init; } = Count > 0 ? Count : throw new ArgumentException(); }` enforces on primary-constructor calls — but `someRecord with { Count = -1 }` bypasses the constructor and lands a -1. **High-signal failure (cited from consuming-pr-3)**: `PageSize` record with primary constructor validating `> 0` but caller did `existing with { PageSize = 0 }` and the resulting record carried 0 past the validation gate. **Fix patterns**: (a) duplicate the invariant in the property initializer: `public required int Count { get; init; } = ValidateCount(Count);` (works for `with` too, because `init` setter runs the validator); (b) make the property fully-`get`-only (drop `init`) — `with`-expression cannot then mutate; (c) document the invariant-bypass risk in XML doc AND ensure no code path uses `with`. **Acceptable (do NOT flag)**: records with no invariants beyond null-check; records whose `init` setters explicitly re-run the same validator that the primary constructor used; records intentionally allowing invariant violation for builder-pattern progressive construction (document the staging contract). **Audit method**: for every NEW record / class with a primary constructor doing validation, check whether any property uses `init` (or `set`). If yes, verify the property's setter ALSO runs the validator OR the property is `get`-only. |  | MEDIUM |
| stale-read-fix-coverage-completeness | review-pass-only | {} | For every NEW fix in the diff that addresses a stale-read bug (race between writer and reader; cache-eviction visibility delay): verify the fix is applied to ALL methods that read the same field, not just the reader that surfaced the bug. Stale-reads typically have multiple consumers (UI getter, command-evaluator, decision-maker, telemetry); fixing only one means the others still race. **High-signal failure (cited from consuming-pr-3)**: a `LastValidatedAt` field was stale on the UI getter; fix added a `Volatile.Read` on the UI getter — but `IsValid()` (the command-evaluator) ALSO reads the same field with plain access and continued returning stale validity in production. **Fix patterns**: (a) apply the fix to ALL read sites: grep for every reader of the field; add the same memory-barrier / volatile / cache-invalidation; (b) refactor the field to a thread-safe wrapper (`ImmutableInterlocked.InterlockedExchange`); (c) document a SINGLE reader API and consolidate all consumers through it. **Acceptable (do NOT flag)**: read sites that genuinely tolerate stale reads (best-effort telemetry, debugging-only views); read sites in the same thread as the writer (no race possible). **Audit method**: for every NEW stale-read fix in the diff (look for `Volatile.Read`/`Interlocked` additions, lock acquisitions added around reads), grep the codebase for every other reader of the same field. Verify each reader received the same fix or has an explicit acceptable-stale annotation. |  | MEDIUM |
| pr-description-api-surface-sync | review-pass-only | {} | For every NEW PR description content that names a public API surface (type kind: "introduces a record / class / struct / interface", method signature, parameter list, return type): verify the description matches the actual implementation. PR descriptions often describe an INTENT that the implementation evolved past — a description saying "introduces `record FilterRecord(string Name, ...)`" while the impl declares `public class FilterRecord` is wrong on type kind. **High-signal failure (cited from consuming-pr-3)**: PR description said "introduces a `record FilterRule`" but the implementation declared `public class FilterRule` — bot caught the mismatch. **Fix patterns**: (a) re-read the PR description against the diff before requesting review; (b) extract API surface claims from the description into a checklist + verify each claim against `git diff`; (c) auto-generate API surface tables in the PR description from the diff (slow but exact). **Acceptable (do NOT flag)**: PR descriptions that explicitly use approximate language ("a record-like type") for marketing summary; descriptions whose API summary has been intentionally simplified for readability AND the simplification is documented. **Audit method**: extract every "introduces X" / "adds method Y" / "the new Z" claim from the PR description; for each, grep the diff for the matching declaration; verify type kind (record/class/struct/interface), name, signature, parameter list, and return type all match. |  | MEDIUM |
| cross-pr-api-state-propagation | review-pass-only | {} | For every NEW PR whose pre-implementation panel review assumes a base-class / interface API surface from an EARLIER MERGED PR: verify the assumption against the CURRENT state of the merged PR, not the state AT THE TIME the earlier PR was reviewed. Earlier PRs sometimes have post-merge edits (virtual override additions, parameter additions, default-value tweaks) that subsequent PRs need to re-acknowledge. **High-signal failure (cited from consuming-pr-4)**: the pre-impl panel assumed `BaseTabComponent` exposed methods X, Y, Z (as defined when PR-3 was reviewed). After PR-3 merged, a follow-up commit added virtual overrides + a new param. PR-4's pre-impl panel never re-checked the merged base-class state → impl missed the new param. **Fix patterns**: (a) at pre-impl panel time, refresh the base-class assumption against `main` HEAD (not the pre-merge state); (b) include base-class signature snapshots in the panel context; (c) note in the PR description "depends on PR-X merged state as of <SHA>" and re-verify against that SHA. **Acceptable (do NOT flag)**: panels reviewing self-contained features with no cross-PR API dependency; PRs whose base-class assumption is explicitly snapshotted via referenced commit SHA. **Audit method**: for every PR following a merge of a related PR, list the assumed base-class / interface surfaces from the new PR's pre-impl panel. For each, fetch the current `main` state of the surface and diff against the panel's assumption. Mismatches = flag. |  | MEDIUM |
| async-wrapper-redundant-upcast | review-pass-only | {} | For every NEW expression-bodied async method in the diff that awaits a SINGLE inner `Task<T>` and returns `Task` (upcast): verify whether the `async`/`await` keywords + state-machine allocation are necessary. `public Task DoXAsync() => await _inner.GetAsync();` allocates a state machine for no reason — `Task<T>` upcasts implicitly to `Task` without await. Drop the keywords: `public Task DoXAsync() => _inner.GetAsync();`. **High-signal failure (cited from consuming-pr-4)**: `public async Task SaveAsync() => await _backing.SaveAsync();` — pointless allocation. **Fix patterns**: (a) drop `async`/`await` when the wrapper is a pass-through: `public Task SaveAsync() => _backing.SaveAsync();`; (b) keep `async` ONLY when the wrapper does pre/post-work, exception translation, or merges multiple awaits. **Acceptable (do NOT flag)**: wrappers that translate exceptions in a try/catch (drop `await` = drop the catch); wrappers that do post-await work (`var x = await inner(); return DoMore(x);`); wrappers whose inner returns `ValueTask<T>` and the outer signature requires `Task` (explicit `.AsTask()` is clearer than `await`). **Audit method**: for every NEW expression-bodied `async Task Foo() => await Bar()` pattern in the diff, verify the `await` is needed. If the wrapper does no pre/post work, flag. |  | LOW |
| await-discard-redundant | review-pass-only | {} | For every NEW `_ = await SomeTaskReturningCall(...)` pattern in the diff: verify the discard is necessary. `await` on a `Task<T>` already discards the result implicitly when the surrounding context doesn't capture it; the explicit `_ =` is noise. **High-signal failure (cited from consuming-pr-4)**: `_ = await client.SendAsync(req);` where the response is genuinely not needed — drop the `_ =`. **Fix patterns**: drop the `_ =`: `await client.SendAsync(req);`. **Acceptable (do NOT flag)**: discard on a `Task` return (no result, so `_ =` is harmless cosmetic); discard required by a static-analysis rule (some teams configure CA-warning-as-error on un-awaited Task that's actually awaited but discard-pattern-needed); `_ = SomeSyncMethod()` for fire-and-forget-style explicit discard of non-Task value (different pattern, intentional). **Audit method**: grep `_\s*=\s*await\s` in NEW code. For each, verify the surrounding context: if the result is not later used AND no static-analysis rule requires the discard, flag. |  | LOW |
| dictionary-ctor-over-linq-todictionary | review-pass-only | {} | For every NEW `.ToDictionary(kvp => kvp.Key, kvp => kvp.Value)` (or equivalent identity-extraction `ToDictionary` call) over an `IDictionary<K,V>` / `IReadOnlyDictionary<K,V>` source: replace with `new Dictionary<K,V>(source)` constructor — avoids the LINQ enumerator + lambda allocation. **High-signal failure (cited from consuming-pr-4)**: `var copy = original.ToDictionary(kvp => kvp.Key, kvp => kvp.Value);` — when `original` is already an `IDictionary`, `new Dictionary<K,V>(original)` is shorter, faster, and allocates less. **Fix patterns**: (a) `new Dictionary<K,V>(source)`; (b) `new Dictionary<K,V>(source, source.Comparer)` when source is `Dictionary<K,V>` and the comparer matters; (c) `source.ToImmutableDictionary()` when an immutable result is wanted. **Acceptable (do NOT flag)**: `ToDictionary` with NON-identity selectors (`source.ToDictionary(x => x.Id, x => x.Value)`); `ToDictionary` over an `IEnumerable<T>` that's not already keyed (the LINQ form IS the simplest); `ToDictionary` with an explicit non-default comparer not exposed by the constructor. **Audit method**: grep `\.ToDictionary\(\s*kvp\s*=>\s*kvp\.Key\s*,\s*kvp\s*=>\s*kvp\.Value\s*\)` in NEW code. For each match, verify the source is an `IDictionary`/`IReadOnlyDictionary`. Flag with the `new Dictionary<K,V>(source)` suggestion. |  | LOW |
| interpolated-string-no-placeholder | review-pass-only | {} | For every NEW `$"..."` interpolated string in the diff that contains NO `{}` placeholders: verify the `$` prefix is necessary. C# allows `$"literal"` for backwards-compat / interpolated-string-handler contexts (e.g., `ILogger` overloads) but it's a code smell otherwise — reader expects an interpolation, finds none. **Fix patterns**: (a) drop the `$` prefix: `"literal"`; (b) if the API REQUIRES an interpolated-string-handler form (e.g., `ILogger.LogInformation` overloads that take `LogInterpolatedStringHandler`), add a meaningful interpolation: `$"completed (count: {count})"`. **Acceptable (do NOT flag)**: cases where the API genuinely requires `FormattableString` / `IFormattable` and a placeholder-free interpolation is documented intent; cases where the `$` is part of a known compiler-optimization opt-in. **Audit method**: grep `\$"[^"{}]*"` (interpolated string with no `{` and no `}`) in NEW code. For each match, verify the receiver API doesn't require interpolated-string-handler form; if not required, flag. |  | LOW |

---

## FP-1: parent-namespace-not-in-scope

**Technical claim**: a child namespace requires an explicit `using` directive to reference an identifier declared in its parent namespace.

**Why FP**: Per C# spec ECMA-334 § 13.8 (Namespace declarations) / C# 7 spec § 3.8 (Namespace and type names), simple-name lookup walks enclosing namespaces from innermost to outermost. A type declared in a parent namespace is visible from any descendant namespace without a `using` directive. Build green = lookup succeeded = claim is wrong.

**Recurrence pattern**: tends to recur across multiple PRs touching the same parent/child namespace pair; Copilot's pattern matcher doesn't apply the C# language rule consistently. Phrasing varies ("isn't in scope", "won't compile", "requires explicit using") — match on technical claim, not exact wording.

**Canonical dismissal template**:
> False positive — C# child namespaces have implicit access to parent-namespace identifiers per ECMA-334 § 13.8 / C# 7 spec § 3.8. The file's namespace is a descendant of the target type's namespace; no `using` directive is required. Build green at the cited commit confirms the lookup succeeds. Recurring FP per `pattern-catalog.md` FP-1.

**Mitigation candidates** (deferred for low FP cost): add explicit `using <parent-namespace>;` per file (1 line per file; reduces FP but pollutes the using list); move type into descendant namespace if domain semantics permit (forces a namespace where it doesn't semantically belong). Status: project-by-project decision.

## FP-2: await-using-on-idisposable-only-type

**Technical claim**: the type returned at the use site implements `IDisposable` but NOT `IAsyncDisposable`, so `await using` will not compile.

**Why FP**: agent must verify the actual type at the use site. Specifically:
- `Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection.ServiceProvider` — implements BOTH `IDisposable` AND `IAsyncDisposable` since .NET Core 3.0 (2019). See [ServiceProvider class reference](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.extensions.dependencyinjection.serviceprovider).
- `System.IO.Stream` — implements BOTH since .NET Core 3.0. See [Stream.DisposeAsync](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/api/system.io.stream.disposeasync).
- `System.Threading.SemaphoreSlim` and `System.Net.Http.HttpClient` are `IDisposable`-only as of .NET 8; do NOT add them to the dismissal list.

Build evidence: if `await using var x = ...` compiles, the type implements `IAsyncDisposable`. Period.

**Recurrence pattern**: Copilot's pattern matcher appears to have stale framework knowledge about which BCL types implement `IAsyncDisposable`. Tends to recur in clusters (all N CLI command files in a single review batch).

**Canonical dismissal template**:
> False positive — `<TypeName>` implements both `IDisposable` AND `IAsyncDisposable` since `<framework-version>` (cite: `<docs-url>`). Project targets `<consuming-project-tfm>`. `await using` compiles correctly. Build green at the cited commit confirms. Recurring FP per `pattern-catalog.md` FP-2.

**Mitigation candidates**: none worth pursuing. Fix is on Copilot's side. Optional: explicit type annotation at the use site (`await using ServiceProvider sp = ...`) makes the interface implementation more obvious to Copilot's matcher — may reduce FP recurrence.

## FP-3: readonly-field-cannot-be-out-parameter-in-ctor

**Technical claim**: passing a `readonly` instance field as an `out` parameter is illegal C# / will not compile.

**Why FP**: C# language rule (ECMA-334 §15.5.3 / Roslyn `CheckIsValidReceiverForVariableOrError`): `readonly` fields are writable inside the declaring class's instance constructor (and the declaring struct's instance constructor) — they are in a *definite-assignment* context identical to direct assignment, so they can be passed as `out`/`ref` arguments from constructor body. The restriction (`CS0192` / `CS0199`) applies OUTSIDE the constructor. Pattern in question:

```csharp
public sealed class Foo
{
    private readonly IService _service;

    public Foo()
    {
        // LEGAL — assignment via out happens inside the declaring class's ctor.
        SomeHelper.Provide(out _service);
    }
}
```

Build evidence: if `private readonly X _field; public Foo() { Bar(out _field); }` compiles, the language rule was satisfied. Period.

**Recurrence pattern**: Copilot's reviewer matches on "readonly" + "out" without distinguishing ctor scope from method scope. The same FP triggers any time a test fixture uses a helper like `AddBannerSubstitutes(out var ...)` to initialize multiple readonly fields in one call.

**Canonical dismissal template**:
> False positive — C# permits passing readonly fields as `out` parameters inside the declaring class's instance constructor (definite-assignment context per ECMA-334 §15.5.3). The restriction (`CS0192`/`CS0199`) applies only outside the constructor. Build green + tests pass at the cited commit confirm the code compiles. Recurring FP per `pattern-catalog.md` FP-3.

**Mitigation candidates**: none necessary; the pattern is idiomatic for test fixtures that use a helper method to construct + assign multiple substitutes in one call. Optional: a `(Foo, Bar, Baz) tuple` return shape on the helper would sidestep the FP at the cost of two extra lines per fixture and worse named-out-param discoverability — typically not worth it.

---

## Catalog maintenance

After each PR final state (converged-to-zero OR merged OR closed-with-deferred-findings-tracker):

1. Append findings to the global `data/findings.csv` (per its schema).
2. Re-run the regex classifier against the corpus. If a NEW pattern appears with frequency ≥3 across the corpus, propose adding to this catalog (PR to CopilotInstructions) with abstract phrasing.
3. If a pattern's hit rate is decaying (fewer hits round-over-round), demote it from high-frequency battery to lower-frequency section. Demotions require ≥2 consecutive rounds of zero-hits to confirm decay.
4. Update FP entries if Copilot raises a new FP worth deterring (≥2 recurrences + source-grounded dismissal).
5. Commit catalog updates to `CopilotInstructions/<branch>`. Catalog is project-deidentified; no per-project data ever lands here.
